findings.dimension: NYU Stern Full-Time MBA Class Profile & Admissions Statistics (Class of 2026 and Class of 2027)
findings.summary: NYU Stern publishes a detailed Full-Time MBA class profile. The two most recent entering cohorts are the Class of 2026 (entered fall 2024) and the Class of 2027 (entered fall 2025, the current published profile as of 2026). Both years are reported and labeled below. The dominant story is a multi-year application surge (3,075 → 4,550 → 4,933 across the Classes of 2025, 2026, 2027), a record-high average GMAT (10th edition) climbing 732 → 733 → 737, a steadily falling acceptance rate (~31% → 25% → 24%), and a shrinking class size (352 → 336). For the Class of 2027, GRE submission hit a school high (27%) and finance backgrounds spiked sharply (22% → 31%) while consulting and tech declined. Average GPA has held at a record 3.64 for two straight years, and average work experience sits around 5 years. Women fell from 47% (2026) to 45% (2027), while international representation rose from 40% to 43%. NOTE: NYU Stern reports GMAT under both the legacy 10th Edition (avg 737 for 2027) and the new GMAT Focus Edition (avg 682 for 2027) — these are NOT comparable scales, and the predictor must not conflate them. A key data-hygiene caveat: a "25%" acceptance rate that circulates for the Class of 2027 comes from U.S. News and differs from the 24% figure Stern itself reports from its own admissions funnel (4,933 apps / 1,161 admits).

findings.claim: Class of 2027 (current published profile, entered fall 2025): 4,933 applications, 1,161 admits, 336 enrolled, 24% admission rate.
findings.evidence: Official Stern class profile page lists '4933 Applications', '1161 Admits', '24% Admission Rate', '336' enrolled for the Class of 2027. Poets&Quants and Clear Admit both confirm 4,933 apps, 1,161 admits (a school record for admits), 336 enrolled, 24% rate. P&Q notes yield fell to 28.9% from 30.9%.

findings.claim: Class of 2026 (entered fall 2024): 4,550 applications, 1,141 admits, 352 enrolled, ~25% admission rate (Clear Admit: 25.1%).
findings.evidence: Clear Admit Class of 2026 profile reports 4,550 applications (vs 3,075 prior year), 1,141 admits, 25.1% admission rate, 352 enrolled (vs 327 prior year). Target Test Prep and P&Q corroborate 4,550 apps and 352 class size for the 2026 cohort.

findings.claim: Acceptance-rate and application trend over the last 3 cycles is clearly directional: applications RISING, acceptance rate FALLING, class size SHRINKING. Class of 2025: ~3,075 apps / 966 admits / 327 enrolled / ~31% rate. Class of 2026: 4,550 / 1,141 / 352 / ~25%. Class of 2027: 4,933 / 1,161 / 336 / 24%.
findings.evidence: P&Q states apps rose 60% over two years (from 3,075 for the Class of 2025 to 4,933 for 2027) and the 2027 rate of 24% is 'down from 25% prior year.' Clear Admit Class of 2026 profile cites 4,550 vs 3,075 prior year. Search-sourced Class of 2025 figures: 3,075 applicants, 966 admits (~31%), 327 enrolled, avg GMAT 732, GPA 3.61. The 2025 admit/rate figures come from secondary aggregation, not the official page, so confidence is medium for that specific year.

findings.claim: Class of 2027 average GMAT is a RECORD HIGH 737 on the legacy 10th Edition (mid-80% range 690–760); the GMAT Focus Edition average is 682 (mid-80% range 645–725). These are two different scales and must not be merged.
findings.evidence: Official Stern page: 'GMAT (10th Edition) Average 737, 80% Range 690–760' and 'GMAT Focus Edition Average 682, 80% Range 645–725.' P&Q calls 737 'a school record, up from 733.' Target Test Prep lists 10th-edition full range 660–770 and Focus full range 595–775.

findings.claim: Class of 2026 average GMAT was 733 (matching the then-record), with a mid-80% range of 690–760 (full range cited 650–780).
findings.evidence: Clear Admit Class of 2026 profile: 'Average GMAT 733 (matching school record high), GMAT Range 650–780.' Search aggregation confirms 733 average and 690–760 mid-80%. Note: this 733 is on the legacy 10th-edition scale, consistent with the 737 reported the following year.

findings.claim: GRE: Class of 2027 averages 164 Quant / 163 Verbal (combined ~327). Class of 2026 averaged 164 Quant / 164 Verbal. GRE submission share is rising and hit a school high of 27% for the Class of 2027.
findings.evidence: Official Stern page (Class of 2027): 'GRE Quantitative Average 164, Verbal Average 163.' Clear Admit Class of 2026: 'Average GRE Quant 164, Verbal 164.' P&Q: GRE average 327 and notes 27% submitted GRE, 'a school high.' Target Test Prep adds GRE Quant mid-80% 160–170 and Verbal mid-80% 158–168 for 2027.

findings.claim: Test-type distribution. Class of 2027: 39% GMAT, 27% GRE, 6% Executive Assessment, 5% MCAT/LSAT/DAT, plus test waivers (Stern page shows ~28% waivers; Clear Admit breaks this into 7% waiver-eligible and 16% secured waiver). Class of 2026: 45% GMAT, 23% GRE, 8% EA, 5% MCAT/LSAT/DAT, 6% secured waiver, 13% waiver-eligible.
findings.evidence: Official Stern page (2027): GMAT 39%, GRE 27%, EA 6%, Test Waivers ~28%. Clear Admit (2027): '39% GMAT, 27% GRE, 6% EA, 5% MCAT/LSAT/DAT, 7% test-waiver eligible, 16% secured waiver.' Clear Admit (2026): GMAT 45%, GRE 23%, EA 8%, MCAT/LSAT/DAT 5%, Test Waiver 6%, Eligible 13%. Trend: GMAT share falling (45%→39%), GRE share rising (23%→27%).

findings.claim: Average undergrad GPA has held at a record 3.64 for both the Class of 2026 and 2027. Class of 2027 mid-80% range is 3.36–3.89 (full range 3.18–4.00); Class of 2026 mid-80% range was 3.43–3.93.
findings.evidence: Official Stern page (2027): 'Undergraduate GPA Average 3.64, 80% Range 3.36–3.89.' Target Test Prep gives full 2027 range 3.18–4.0. Clear Admit (2026): 'Average Undergraduate GPA 3.64 (vs 3.61 prior year),' with search aggregation giving 2026 mid-80% of 3.43–3.93. Prior cycle (Class of 2025) averaged 3.61, so GPA rose then plateaued at 3.64.

findings.claim: Average work experience is ~5 years (Class of 2027: 5.1 years, 98% with work experience; Class of 2026: ~5 years). Class of 2027 average age 28 (range 23–38).
findings.evidence: Official Stern page (2027): '98% Students with Work Experience, 5.1 Average Years of Work Experience.' P&Q: average age 28, range 23–38. Clear Admit (2026) and search aggregation cite 'average work experience 5 years' for the 2026 cohort.

findings.claim: Women: 45% (Class of 2027), DOWN from 47% (Class of 2026), which had itself risen from 43% (Class of 2025). International citizenship: 43% (2027), UP from 40% (2026). U.S. military veterans/active duty: 9% (2027), down from 11% (2026).
findings.evidence: Official Stern page (2027): 'Women 45%, Students with International Citizenship 43%, US Military Veterans/Active Duty 9%, Countries Represented 43.' Clear Admit (2027): women 45% 'slight decrease from 47%,' international 43% 'up from 40%,' military 9%. Clear Admit (2026): 'Women 47% (vs 43% prior year), International Citizens 40%, Military/Active Duty 11%, Countries Represented 50.'

findings.claim: Undergraduate major breakdown. Class of 2027: Business 30%, Engineering/Math/Science 25%, Economics 19%, Social Science 16%, Humanities/Arts/Other 10%. Class of 2026: Business 32%, Engineering/Math/Science 23%, Economics 18%, Social Sciences 15%, Humanities/Arts/Other 12%.
findings.evidence: Official Stern page (2027): 'Business 30%, Engineering, Math, Science 25%, Economics 19%, Social Science 16%, Humanities, Arts, Other 10%.' Clear Admit (2026): 'Business 32%, Engineering/Math/Science 23%, Economics 18%, Social Sciences 15%, Humanities/Arts/Other 12%.' Slight shift toward STEM and economics, away from pure business.

findings.claim: Pre-MBA industry breakdown shows a notable FINANCE SPIKE for the Class of 2027. 2027: Financial Services/Industries 31%, Consulting 15%, Technology 8%, Entertainment/Media 6%, Military/Government 5% (plus Law/Nonprofit/Healthcare ~4% each, Real Estate 3%). 2026: Financial Services 22%, Consulting 19%, Technology 10%, Military/Government 7%, Consumer Products/Retail 6%.
findings.evidence: Official Stern page (2027): 'Financial Industries 31%, Consulting 15%, Technology 8%.' P&Q (2027): 'Financial Services 31% (major spike from 22%), Consulting 15% (down from 19%), Technology 8% (down from 10%), Entertainment/Media 6%, Military/Government 5%.' Clear Admit (2026): 'Financial Services 22%, Consulting 19%, Technology 10%, Military/Government 7%, Consumer Products/Retail 6%.' Trend: finance up sharply, consulting and tech down.

findings.claim: U.S. minority / underrepresented-minority representation declined for the Class of 2027. 2027: U.S. minorities 45%, underrepresented 18%, LGBTQ+ 10%. 2026: U.S. minorities 46%, underrepresented 22%, LGBTQ+ 11%.
findings.evidence: Clear Admit (2027): 'U.S. Minorities 45%, Underrepresented 18%, Asian American 24%, Hispanic/Latino 13%, African American/Black 5%, Multi-race 4%, LGBTQ+ 10%.' Clear Admit (2026): 'U.S. Minority 46%, Historically Underrepresented 22% (5% increase from prior year), Asian American 25%, Hispanic/Latino 12%, LGBTQ+ 11%.' Underrepresented minority share fell 22%→18% year over year.

findings.claim: DATA CAVEAT: A '25%' acceptance rate sometimes attached to the Class of 2027 is a U.S. News figure, not Stern's own self-reported funnel rate of 24% (4,933 apps / 1,161 admits). Predictor should use 24% as Stern's reported 2027 rate and treat 25% as the U.S. News/cohort-mismatched alternative.
findings.evidence: Target Test Prep explicitly warns the acceptance-rate stat 'is instead drawn from the most recent U.S. News data and may not refer to the same cohort as other data listed here.' Menlo Coaching's profile and acceptance-rate pages cite '25%' sourced to 'NYU Stern, class of 2027 data & U.S. News business school data.' The official Stern page and Clear Admit both state 24% from the actual 2027 funnel.

findings.benchmarks: QUANTITATIVE SPINE (label each by cycle). CLASS OF 2027 (entered fall 2025, current published profile as of 2026): Applications 4,933 | Admits 1,161 | Enrolled 336 | Admission rate 24% (Stern self-reported); 25% per U.S. News/Menlo | Yield 28.9% | Avg GMAT 737 (10th Edition, RECORD; mid-80% 690–760; full ~660–770) | Avg GMAT Focus 682 (mid-80% 645–725; full ~595–775) | Avg GRE 164Q/163V (~327 combined) | GRE Q mid-80% 160–170, GRE V mid-80% 158–168 | Avg GPA 3.64 (mid-80% 3.36–3.89; full 3.18–4.00) | Test mix: GMAT 39%, GRE 27%, EA 6%, MCAT/LSAT/DAT 5%, waiver-eligible 7%, secured waiver 16% | Avg work exp 5.1 yrs (98% with experience) | Avg age 28 (range 23–38) | Women 45% | International 43% | Countries 43 | Military/vet 9% | LGBTQ+ 10% | U.S. minorities 45% (underrepresented 18%) | Majors: Business 30%, Eng/Math/Sci 25%, Econ 19%, Social Science 16%, Humanities/Arts/Other 10% | Industries: Financial 31%, Consulting 15%, Tech 8%, Entertainment/Media 6%, Military/Gov 5%. CLASS OF 2026 (entered fall 2024): Applications 4,550 | Admits 1,141 | Enrolled 352 | Admission rate 25.1% | Avg GMAT 733 (mid-80% 690–760; full 650–780) | Avg GRE 164Q/164V | Avg GPA 3.64 (mid-80% 3.43–3.93) | Test mix: GMAT 45%, GRE 23%, EA 8%, MCAT/LSAT/DAT 5%, waiver 6%, eligible 13% | Avg work exp ~5 yrs | Women 47% | International 40% | Countries 50 | Military/vet 11% | LGBTQ+ 11% | U.S. minorities 46% (underrepresented 22%) | Majors: Business 32%, Eng/Math/Sci 23%, Econ 18%, Social Sciences 15%, Humanities/Arts/Other 12% | Industries: Financial 22%, Consulting 19%, Tech 10%, Military/Gov 7%, Consumer/Retail 6%. CLASS OF 2025 (context/trend, mostly secondary-sourced): Applications ~3,075 | Admits ~966 | Enrolled 327 | Rate ~31% | Avg GMAT 732 | Avg GPA 3.61 | Women 43% | International 48% | Countries 46. THREE-CYCLE TRENDS: Apps 3,075→4,550→4,933 (RISING, +60% over two years); Rate ~31%→25%→24% (FALLING); Class size 327→352→336; GMAT (10th ed) 732→733→737 (RISING, record); GPA 3.61→3.64→3.64 (rose then plateaued); GRE share 23%→27% (rising); Women 43%→47%→45%; International 48%→40%→43%; Finance share 22%→31% (2026→2027, spiking).
predictorImplications: Use Class of 2027 as the primary baseline (most recent published profile as of 2026): avg GMAT 737 on the legacy 10th-Edition scale, avg GPA 3.64, 24% admission rate. The predictor must keep the two GMAT scales separate — a 682 Focus Edition average is NOT a drop from 737; they are different scales. Map applicant Focus scores against the 682 / 645–725 band and legacy scores against 737 / 690–760.

Treat the admission rate as ~24% (Stern self-reported) and falling. Over three cycles it compressed from ~31% to 24% even as the class shrank, so the school is materially more selective than its sub-30% headline of two years ago. A predictor anchored to older ~30% rates will overstate odds.

The mid-80% GMAT band (690–760, 10th ed) and GPA band (3.36–3.89, full 3.18–4.00) define the competitive core. Sub-690 GMAT or sub-3.36 GPA applicants sit below the 10th percentile and need offsetting strengths; the 4.00 GPA ceiling and 760+ scores represent the top decile.

GRE is increasingly accepted and rising in share (27% for 2027, a school high) — a strong GRE (≈164Q/163V, ~327 combined) is fully competitive and should not be penalized versus GMAT in the model. Roughly a third of the class now uses GRE, EA, other exams, or waivers, so the predictor should not assume a GMAT for every applicant.

Industry fit matters and is shifting: finance jumped to 31% of the 2027 class (from 22%) while consulting (15%) and tech (8%) shrank. A finance-background applicant is now entering the single largest and growing pool (more internal competition), whereas the relative scarcity of some non-traditional backgrounds could be modeled as a modest differentiator.

Demographic levers moved against the prior trend for 2027: women fell to 45% and underrepresented minorities to 18% (from 47% and 22%), while international rose to 43%. The predictor should avoid assuming continued monotonic gains in women/URM representation and should weight international applicants against a 43% international share and 43 countries.
Work experience clusters tightly around 5.1 years / age 28 (range 23–38); applicants far outside roughly 3–8 years of experience are atypical and should carry an age/experience-fit adjustment.

Flag the 24% vs 25% acceptance-rate discrepancy in any output: 24% is Stern's own funnel math, 25% is U.S. News and may reference a different cohort. Hard-code 24% for the 2027 cycle to avoid double-counting an inflated denominator.
redFlags: GMAT scale conflation risk: Stern reports BOTH a 10th-Edition average (737) and a GMAT Focus Edition average (682) for the same Class of 2027. These are different scoring scales and are not interchangeable. Any predictor that treats 682 and 737 as comparable, or that compares an applicant's Focus score to the 737 figure, will be badly miscalibrated.

Acceptance-rate source mismatch: 24% (Stern self-reported, from 4,933 apps / 1,161 admits) vs 25% (U.S. News, as surfaced by Menlo Coaching and flagged by Target Test Prep as possibly a different cohort). Do not average them; they measure slightly different things.

Class of 2025 admit count (~966) and ~31% rate are aggregated from secondary sources (search results / Clear Admit context), not pulled cleanly from an official Stern Class of 2025 page in this research pass — confidence medium. The 3,075 application figure and 327 class size are well-corroborated; the exact admit count and rate for 2025 should be re-verified before being used as a hard anchor.

GPA mid-80% ranges differ slightly by source and year (2027: 3.36–3.89 official vs full range 3.18–4.00 from TTP; 2026: 3.43–3.93 from aggregation). Use the official Stern mid-80% (3.36–3.89) as the primary band and treat the full range as endpoints only.

Minor GRE Verbal discrepancy: Class of 2027 Verbal average is 163 (official Stern page) but the prior Class of 2026 was 164 — a 1-point difference that is real, not an error, but easy to mis-transcribe across years.

Waiver-percentage reporting is inconsistent between the official page (which shows an aggregate ~28% 'test waivers' for 2027) and Clear Admit (which splits it into 7% waiver-eligible + 16% secured waiver). The components do not cleanly sum to the headline; use Clear Admit's breakdown but note the labeling ambiguity.

Several 'class profile' aggregator pages (GMAC, BusinessBecause, gmatclub, admitstudio, mim-essay) were surfaced but NOT individually fetched/verified in this pass; they are not cited as primary evidence and should not be used as independent confirmation without direct verification.

checks.claim: Class of 2027 (entered fall 2025, current published profile): 4,933 applications, 1,161 admits, 336 enrolled, 24% admission rate.
Class of 2027 yield fell to 28.9% from 30.9% prior year.
checks.corrected: Confirmed and adds context: P&Q states 'Just under 29% of admits enrolled, compared with 30.9% last year and 33.9% in 2025.' So the full three-year yield trend is 33.9% (2025) -> 30.9% (2026) -> ~28.9% (2027). The draft's 28.9%/30.9% step is correct.

checks.claim: 1,161 admits is a school record for admits.
Class of 2026 (entered fall 2024): 4,550 applications, 1,141 admits, 352 enrolled, ~25% admission rate (25.1%).
checks.corrected: Clear Admit states the rate precisely as 25.07% (draft's '25.1%' is a rounding of this); applications 4,550, admits 1,141, enrolled 352 all confirmed.

checks.claim: Class of 2025: ~3,075 apps / 966 admits / 327 enrolled / ~31% rate / GMAT 732 / GPA 3.61 (draft marked medium confidence, admit count/rate flagged for re-verification).
checks.corrected: All figures corroborated by Clear Admit's Class of 2025 profile and restated in Clear Admit's Class of 2026 profile (3,075 applicants, 966 admits, 31% rate, 327 enrolled). These are directly sourced, not loose aggregation - the draft's medium-confidence caveat can be UPGRADED to high for apps/admits/rate/enrolled. (GMAT 732, GPA 3.61 also confirmed.)

checks.claim: Three-cycle trend: applications RISING (3,075->4,550->4,933, +60% over two years), acceptance rate FALLING (~31%->25%->24%), class size 327->352->336.
checks.corrected: P&Q: 'a 60% increase from just two years ago' and acceptance rate 'dipped to 24%, lowest since fall 2021.' Note nuance: the 2026->2027 application jump is only +4% (4,550->4,933) per Clear Admit; the dramatic +60% is driven by the 2025->2026 surge. Class size went UP then DOWN (327->352->336), as the draft states.

checks.claim: Class of 2027 average GMAT is record-high 737 (10th Edition, 80% range 690-760); GMAT Focus Edition average 682 (80% range 645-725); these are different scales.
GMAT full ranges: 10th Edition 660-770, Focus Edition 595-775 (Class of 2027).
Class of 2026 average GMAT was 733 (matching then-record), full range 650-780.
checks.corrected: Clear Admit confirms average 733 'matching school record' and range 650-780. The draft also cites an '80% range 690-760' for 2026 from aggregation; the only range Clear Admit publishes is the 650-780 full range - the 690-760 80% band for 2026 was not independently confirmed in this pass (low confidence on that sub-detail), though 690-760 IS the official 80% band for 2027.

checks.claim: GRE: Class of 2027 averages 164 Quant / 163 Verbal; Class of 2026 averaged 164 Quant / 164 Verbal; GRE submission rose to a school-high 27% (from 23%).
checks.corrected: Official Stern page: GRE Q 164 / V 163 (2027). Clear Admit 2026: Q 164 / V 164. P&Q: '27% of students submitted GRE scores, a new school high-water mark, up from 23% last year.' The 1-point Verbal drop (164->163) is real, as the draft notes. GRE 80% ranges (Q 160-170, V 158-168) confirmed via Target Test Prep.

checks.claim: Test-type distribution. 2027: GMAT 39%, GRE 27%, EA 6%, MCAT/LSAT/DAT 5%, waiver-eligible 7%, secured waiver 16%. 2026: GMAT 45%, GRE 23%, EA 8%, MCAT/LSAT/DAT 5%, secured waiver 6%, waiver-eligible 13%.
checks.corrected: Both years confirmed by official Stern page (2027) and Clear Admit (both years). Note: the draft's parenthetical that the official page shows an aggregate '~28% test waivers' for 2027 is imprecise - the official page actually lists 'Test Waiver: 16%' and 'NYU Test Waiver: 7%' as SEPARATE line items (these are the secured-waiver and waiver-eligible buckets, summing to 23%, not 28%). The '~28%' aggregate framing in the draft's evidence is not supported by the official page; use the 16%/7% split. The component claims themselves are correct.

checks.claim: Average undergrad GPA 3.64 for both 2026 and 2027; 2027 80% range 3.36-3.89 (full 3.18-4.00); 2026 80% range 3.43-3.93.
checks.corrected: GPA 3.64 (both years) and 2027 80% range 3.36-3.89 confirmed by official Stern page; full 2027 range 3.18-4.00 confirmed by Target Test Prep. The 2026 80% band of 3.43-3.93 was NOT independently confirmed in this pass (Clear Admit 2026 publishes the 3.64 average and 3.61 prior-year average but no 80% band); treat that sub-range as low confidence. Prior-year (2025) GPA 3.61 confirmed.

checks.claim: Average work experience ~5 years (2027: 5.1 yrs, 98% with experience; 2026: ~5 yrs). 2027 average age 28 (range 23-38).
checks.corrected: Official Stern page: '98% Students with Work Experience, 5.1 Average Years of Work Experience,' average age 28 (23-38). Work-experience range cited as 0-14 years. Clear Admit 2026: average work experience 5 years. All confirmed.

checks.claim: Women: 45% (2027) down from 47% (2026), which rose from 43% (2025). International: 43% (2027) up from 40% (2026). Military: 9% (2027) down from 11% (2026).
Countries represented: 43 (2027), 50 (2026).

Undergraduate major breakdown. 2027: Business 30%, Eng/Math/Sci 25%, Econ 19%, Social Science 16%, Humanities/Arts/Other 10%. 2026: Business 32%, Eng/Math/Sci 23%, Econ 18%, Social Sciences 15%, Humanities/Arts/Other 12%.

Pre-MBA industry, with finance spike for 2027. 2027: Financial 31%, Consulting 15%, Tech 8%, Entertainment/Media 6%, Military/Gov 5%. 2026: Financial 22%, Consulting 19%, Tech 10%, Military/Gov 7%, Consumer/Retail 6%.
checks.corrected: All confirmed. P&Q: 'Financial services...surged to 31%, up sharply from 22% in the Class of 2026.' Note the 2027 finance figure is labeled 'Financial Industries 31%' on the official page; 2026 figures from Clear Admit. Consulting 19%->15% and Tech 10%->8% declines confirmed.

checks.claim: U.S. minority / URM. 2027: U.S. minorities 45%, underrepresented 18%, Asian American 24%, Hispanic/Latino 13%, Black 5%, Multi-race 4%, LGBTQ+ 10%. 2026: U.S. minorities 46%, underrepresented 22%, Asian American 25%, Hispanic/Latino 12%, LGBTQ+ 11%.
checks.corrected: 2027 figures fully confirmed by Clear Admit. For 2026, U.S. minorities 46%, underrepresented 22% (up 5 pts), Asian American 25%, LGBTQ+ 11% confirmed. CAUTION on a parsing artifact: one automated extraction returned 'African American/Black 12%' for 2026, which is almost certainly a mis-parse colliding with the Hispanic/Latino 12% figure - the draft does NOT claim a 2026 Black %, so the draft is unaffected, but do not introduce a 2026 Black % without direct verification. URM decline 22%->18% YoY confirmed.

checks.claim: DATA CAVEAT: A '25%' acceptance rate attached to the Class of 2027 is a U.S. News figure, not Stern's self-reported 24% funnel rate. Target Test Prep explicitly warns the rate 'is instead drawn from the most recent U.S. News data and may not refer to the same cohort'; Menlo Coaching cites 25% sourced to U.S. News.
checks.corrected: The SUBSTANCE is correct and well-sourced: 24% is Stern's self-reported funnel rate (4,933 apps / 1,161 admits), and 25% circulates from U.S. News. P&Q corroborates: '24%, lowest since fall 2021.' HOWEVER the source attribution is SWAPPED. The exact quote 'this stat is instead drawn from the most recent U.S. News data and may not refer to the same cohort as other data listed here' appears on MENLO COACHING's page, not Target Test Prep. The current Target Test Prep page lists the acceptance rate as 24% with NO such U.S. News/cohort disclaimer. Fix: attribute the U.S. News '25%' figure and the cohort-mismatch caveat to Menlo Coaching; do not cite Target Test Prep for that caveat.

verify.netAssessment:
The class-profile dimension is overwhelmingly ACCURATE and well-sourced. I independently re-verified every hard number against the official NYU Stern class profile page plus Clear Admit (2025, 2026, 2027), Poets&Quants, Target Test Prep, and Menlo Coaching. The Class of 2027 figures (4,933 apps / 1,161 admits / 336 enrolled / 24% / GMAT 737 10th-ed / Focus 682 / GPA 3.64 / women 45% / international 43% / finance 31%) and the Class of 2026 figures (4,550 / 1,141 / 352 / 25.07% / GMAT 733 / women 47% / international 40%) are all confirmed exactly. The cycle labeling (2026 = fall 2024, 2027 = fall 2025 current profile) is correct, and the two GMAT scales are correctly kept distinct.

Corrections/refinements: (1) The MOST IMPORTANT fix is a source-attribution error in the data-caveat claim: the verbatim 'drawn from the most recent U.S. News data and may not refer to the same cohort' warning is on MENLO COACHING's page, not Target Test Prep. The current Target Test Prep page shows 24% with no such disclaimer. The caveat's substance (24% Stern self-reported vs 25% U.S. News) is correct and corroborated by P&Q ('24%, lowest since fall 2021'), but the citation must be reattributed to Menlo. (2) The draft's parenthetical that the official Stern page shows an aggregate '~28% test waivers' for 2027 is imprecise - the page lists two separate line items, 'Test Waiver 16%' (secured) and 'NYU Test Waiver 7%' (eligible), which sum to 23%, not 28%; use the 16%/7% split. (3) The Class of 2025 anchor (3,075/966/327/31%) that the draft marked medium-confidence and 'should be re-verified' is in fact directly sourced from Clear Admit's 2025 and 2026 profiles - it can be UPGRADED to high confidence. (4) Minor: the +60% application surge is concentrated in 2025->2026; the 2026->2027 step is only +4%. (5) Two sub-ranges the draft pulled from aggregation - the 2026 GMAT 80% band (690-760) and 2026 GPA 80% band (3.43-3.93) - were NOT independently confirmable here and should be treated as low confidence (the official 80% bands I confirmed are for the 2027 cohort). (6) A likely parsing artifact ('African American/Black 12%' for 2026) should be ignored; the draft does not rely on it. The yield trend (33.9%->30.9%->28.9%) is confirmed. No fabricated numbers were found.

findings.dimension: NYU Stern Full-Time (Two-Year) MBA application components & current requirements — 2026-27 cycle (Fall 2027 entry), the most recent published cycle
findings.summary: For the 2026-27 cycle (Fall 2027 entry), NYU Stern's FT MBA requires three essays: (1) a "Professional Aspirations" essay — "What are your short-term career goals? Why is the Stern MBA the necessary next chapter in your professional story? Please be specific." (500 words max, double-spaced, 12-pt); (2) the "Pick Six" visual/personal-expression essay (single PDF: ≤3-sentence intro + six images + one-sentence caption each); and (3) an optional Additional Information essay (500 words max). IMPORTANT CYCLE NOTE: the "Change: ___ it" essay was a 2025-26 prompt; it was REMOVED for 2026-27, and the previously 150-word Professional Aspirations short answer was expanded into the 500-word essay above — so for 2026-27 there is NO "Change: ___ it" essay on the official Stern essays page. Stern does not use traditional letters of recommendation; instead it requires one EQ Endorsement (from a current supervisor; a second optional endorsement may come from anyone professional or personal, not immediate family). Endorsers answer four substantive questions plus an optional one, and rate the applicant on a 10-competency grid (Exceptional→Poor / Unable to judge). A valid standardized test is required (GMAT/GRE/EA/LSAT/MCAT/DAT accepted equally), with a formal test-waiver request process (April 1, 2027 deadline; ~3-week decision) and an automatic waiver for NYU undergrads with cumulative GPA ≥3.20. Deadlines: R1 Sep 15 2026, R2 Oct 15 2026, R3 Jan 15 2027, R4 Apr 15 2027; R1/R2/R3 initial notifications Dec 1 2026 / Jan 1 2027 / Apr 1 2027, R4 ongoing. predictorImplications: The single biggest 2026-27 change for a predictor model is the essay set: drop any rubric weighting the 350-word 'Change: ___ it' essay — it no longer exists. The load-bearing written essay is now the 500-word Professional Aspirations (short-term goals + why-Stern-now), so goal clarity and program-fit signal should be weighted up versus prior cycles.

The Pick Six visual essay is structurally rigid (exactly 6 images, ≤3-sentence intro, one-sentence caption each, single PDF). A predictor should treat it as a personality/values-fit signal rather than a writing-quality signal, and flag any format non-compliance (wrong image count, missing captions, non-PDF) as a procedural risk.

Stern uses EQ Endorsements, not traditional rec letters — and the required one must come from a CURRENT supervisor. Predictor logic that assumes 2-3 generic recommenders is wrong for Stern; absence of a current-supervisor endorsement (without a strong written explanation) is a meaningful negative signal given Stern's IQ+EQ emphasis.

The endorser rating grid (10 competencies, Exceptional→Poor scale, including a dedicated 'Emotional Intelligence' line) means EQ is explicitly scored. A model should weight interpersonal/EQ evidence in the file (essays, resume, endorsement) more heavily for Stern than for peer schools.

Test policy is REQUIRED (not test-optional) for the FT and Luxury & Retail MBAs, but there is a real waiver path. Model the waiver as a distinct track: NYU-undergrad + GPA ≥3.20 is automatic; everyone else must clear a separate request (April 1, 2027 deadline, ~3-week turnaround, 300-word readiness essay). A blank GMAT/GRE field should not auto-penalize if a waiver was granted.

Interviews are comprehensive and required for admission, with only ~20-25% invited — so an interview invite is itself a strong positive intermediate outcome, and a predictor should treat the post-application stage as a two-step funnel (invite-to-interview, then admit) with the interview invite as a high-information signal.

Round timing matters: R4 (Apr 15, 2027) is restricted to U.S. citizens/permanent residents, so an international applicant's effective last round is R3 (Jan 15, 2027). Encode citizenship × round eligibility to avoid recommending an impossible round.
Fee ($250) and the military/veteran fee waiver are low-friction operational facts; useful for completeness checks but not predictive of admit probability.

findings.dimension: NYU Stern Full-Time MBA standardized test waiver — the real policy and how it affects admit odds
findings.summary: Resolved definitively: There is NO automatic veteran/active-duty test waiver for the NYU Stern FULL-TIME MBA. The automatic military/veteran waiver exists only for the part-time Langone MBA. The widespread "Stern waives the test for vets" claim online conflates the two programs. For the Full-Time (2-year) MBA and Luxury & Retail MBA, the only AUTOMATIC waiver is for NYU undergraduate alumni with a 3.20+ cumulative GPA. Everyone else (including veterans) must submit a by-request discretionary waiver: a separate online form with resume, all transcripts, certifications, and a 300-word essay demonstrating academic readiness, judged by AdCom on rolling basis (decision ~3 weeks; deadline ~April 1). Separately, the Andre Koo Tech MBA is fully test-optional for the 2025-26 cycle — distinct from a waiver. On admit odds: Stern officially states a waiver does not disadvantage you, and mbaMission agrees applying for/being granted a waiver "will not have a negative effect." But named consultancies (Menlo Coaching, Stacy Blackman) strongly advise taking the GMAT/GRE anyway for a top-15 program: a strong score is the single best lever for both admission and merit scholarships, and the Class of 2027 entered with a record 737 average GMAT (10th ed.) / 682 Focus / 327 GRE / 3.64 GPA — meaning most enrolled students DID submit a competitive score. A waiver is genuinely safe only for applicants whose quantitative bona fides are already strong (analytical degree, CFA/CPA, heavy financial-modeling experience); it is risky for early-career, career-changer, or low-quant-rigor profiles. Consultants note scholarship/merit decisions lean heavily on test scores, so a waiver can cost money even when it doesn't cost an admit.

findings.claim: There is NO automatic veteran/active-duty test waiver for the NYU Stern FULL-TIME MBA; the automatic military waiver applies ONLY to the part-time Langone program.
findings.evidence: Stern's part-time/Langone Tests & Waivers page states verbatim: 'Active members of the U.S. military and U.S. veterans can waive the standardized test requirement for the Langone Part-time MBA program' and the waiver 'will be automatically applied to your profile.' The Full-Time MBA standardized-tests page lists NO automatic military waiver — only the NYU-alumni auto-waiver and the discretionary by-request waiver. Direct fetch of both official pages confirmed the military auto-waiver is named specifically and only for Langone Part-time.

findings.claim: The ONLY automatic test waiver for the Full-Time (2-year) MBA is for NYU undergraduate alumni with a 3.20+ cumulative GPA.
findings.evidence: Full-Time MBA page, exact language: 'Applicants who have an undergraduate degree from New York University (including NYU Shanghai and NYU Abu Dhabi) with a final cumulative GPA of 3.20 of higher, will not be required to submit a standardized test score when applying to the Full-time 2-year MBA.' No work-experience, advanced-degree, or business/engineering-major auto-waivers exist for the Full-Time MBA (those business/engineering 3.20+ categories exist for Langone Part-time, not FT).

findings.claim: For all non-NYU-alumni Full-Time MBA applicants (including veterans), the waiver is a by-request, discretionary application — a separate form with resume, all transcripts, certifications, and a 300-word academic-readiness essay.
findings.evidence: Stern: 'If you are unable to prepare for or take a standardized test and can demonstrate academic readiness through other means, you may request a waiver.' Required materials: completed waiver request form, resume, official transcripts (all undergrad/grad institutions), professional certifications, and a brief essay (300-word limit) listing 'coursework completed, certifications held, and professional experience that demonstrates academic readiness for an MBA program.' Eligibility: bachelor's degree must be conferred before submitting; dual-degree applicants are NOT eligible; one waiver request per cycle.

findings.claim: AdCom weighs evidence of quantitative/analytical readiness in granting an FT waiver — analytical/quant degrees, relevant professional experience, and certifications increase approval likelihood.
findings.evidence: Stern: the committee considers 'Strong examples of academic readiness—such as an analytical or quantitative undergraduate or graduate degree, relevant professional experience, or certifications.' Reviewed on a rolling basis with a decision 'within 3 weeks of submission' in most cases; Stern encourages submitting the waiver request at least 3 weeks before the application. Waiver-request deadline is ~April 1 for the cycle (page showed 'Thursday, April 1, 2027' for the 2026-27 cycle).

findings.claim: Accepted tests when not waived are GMAT, GRE, Executive Assessment (EA), LSAT, MCAT, and DAT, with no stated preference among them.
findings.evidence: Full-Time MBA page: accepts 'GMAT, Executive Assessment, GRE, LSAT, MCAT, and DAT' and states 'Stern has no preference for which test applicants include with their application.'

findings.claim: The Andre Koo Tech MBA is fully TEST-OPTIONAL for the 2025-26 cycle — a separate concept from the Full-Time MBA's waiver; the Luxury & Retail MBA still requires a test (or a waiver).
findings.evidence: Stern materials: 'the 2026 Andre Koo Tech MBA program is test optional, and applicants will not be required to submit a standardized test score... but may still do so.' Test-optional applies only to the 2025-26 cycle for the Tech MBA. The Full-Time MBA 'requires a standardized test but offers test waivers for qualified candidates,' and the Luxury & Retail MBA still requires a test. So 'test-optional' and 'waiver' are distinct: only the Tech MBA is truly test-optional.

findings.claim: Stern and mbaMission state that applying for, or being granted, a waiver does NOT negatively affect admission chances — but mbaMission warns it is unstrategic for weak-quant profiles.

But: 'If you have a weak academic record, including a lack of quantitative coursework and/or poor grades... applying for a test waiver would not be strategic, as you are unlikely to have your waiver request approved.' (mbaMission page is 403-blocked to direct fetch; quotes captured via search snippet of the same article.)

findings.claim: Menlo Coaching argues a waiver effectively HURTS odds at top MBA programs and recommends taking the GMAT/GRE anyway; a low score, however, is worse than no score.
findings.evidence: Menlo Coaching: 'if you're applying to top business schools, it's likely not in your best interest to submit your MBA applications without a GMAT or GRE score'; 'a strong test score is an advantage you can't afford to pass up'; 'An applicant for a top full-time MBA should usually aim to submit their application with a test score—even if a waiver is possible.' They also note AdCom would rather admit someone with no score than a below-average score (rankings pressure), and advise trying tutoring before resorting to a waiver. If you make it seem like you just want to avoid taking a test, it can hurt your application, even if you are otherwise a strong candidate.' predictorImplications: Do NOT model NYU Stern as having an automatic veteran/active-duty test waiver for the Full-Time MBA. The automatic military waiver is a Langone Part-time feature only. A predictor that grants veterans an auto-waiver in the FT model will mis-score that population — veterans must go through the same discretionary by-request waiver as everyone else.

The only automatic FT waiver lever is NYU-undergrad alumni with 3.20+ GPA. Encode that as a binary auto-waiver eligibility flag; all other waivers are discretionary and approval-dependent on demonstrated quant readiness.

Treat 'test-optional' (Andre Koo Tech MBA, 2025-26 only) and 'test waiver' (Full-Time/L&R MBA) as separate program-specific features — do not collapse them. Luxury & Retail still requires a test or waiver.

Encode 'waiver vs. competitive score' as a feature that interacts with quant-profile strength rather than as a flat penalty/bonus.

Model scholarship/merit-aid odds as materially lower for waiver/test-optional applicants — test scores are a primary merit lever. Recommend predictor advise a strong GMAT/GRE for any applicant targeting merit money.

Profile-condition the recommendation: waiver is safe for high-quant profiles (analytical/quant degree, CFA/CPA, heavy financial-modeling experience); risky for early-career, career-changers, and qualitative-major / low-quant-rigor profiles, who should take the test.

Surface the FT waiver-request deadline (~April 1) and 3-week rolling review as a timing constraint — applicants must request the waiver well before their round deadline, which the predictor should flag as a gating step distinct from the application itself.
redFlags: Conflicting consultant guidance: mbaMission says a waiver does NOT hurt admit odds; Menlo Coaching effectively says it does at top schools. Both can be true — neutral on a pure admit/deny basis but costly on competitiveness/merit. Do not present either as the sole 'truth.'
