findings.dimension: How the NYU Stern admissions committee actually evaluates applicants — its stated philosophy and what it rewards/penalizes
findings.summary: NYU Stern's evaluation philosophy is built on one explicit doctrine — "IQ + EQ" — meaning the AdCom deliberately weighs emotional/interpersonal intelligence alongside intellectual/analytical horsepower, and treats the two as co-equal rather than EQ being a tiebreaker. This is operationalized through three review lenses ("the Three Cs": classroom, career, character) and a uniquely structured application: a required EQ Endorsement (no other top school has this), the "Pick Six" visual essay, a 350-word "Change: ___ it" brand-aligned essay, a 500-word goals/Why-Stern-Why-NYC essay, and an invitation-only behavioral interview conducted only by trained Stern admissions staff (not alumni/students). The committee uses full holistic review — every application is read in its entirety by multiple reviewers. Leadership through the change is consistent across two AdCom leaders: Isser Gallogly (who built the EQ-focused application starting ~2017-2018 and is Associate Dean of MBA Admissions & Program Innovation) and Lindsay Loyd (Assistant Dean of MBA Admissions, the current operational head, on record June 2025). REWARDS: authenticity, self-awareness (especially candidly naming a weakness), insight over creativity, collaboration/communication, third-party-validated EQ, specificity on goals and Why Stern/Why NYC, and a cohesive personal narrative. PENALIZES: not reading instructions/cutting corners, vague goals, lack of Why-Stern/Why-NYC clarity, no backup plan, surface-level answers, poor judgment/inappropriate language in interview, and inauthenticity (offers rescinded if you didn't write your own essays). Note: most "what they reward/penalize" specifics on essays come from named consultancies interpreting Stern, while the doctrinal core (IQ+EQ, Three Cs, holistic read, EQ endorsement value, self-awareness) is directly on the record from Stern AdCom leadership.

findings.claim: Stern's central admissions doctrine is 'IQ + EQ' — it explicitly weighs emotional/interpersonal intelligence as co-equal to intellectual/analytical ability, and considers it a 'core value' used to build the class.
findings.evidence: Stern's official EQ endorsement page states 'IQ+EQ is a core value of NYU Stern,' seeking candidates with 'both intellectual and interpersonal strengths.' Current AdCom head Lindsay Loyd (Clear Admit, June 19, 2025): 'We strive to build a class and community of individuals who demonstrate IQ + EQ.' Gallogly (P&Q, 2018): 'IQ plus EQ is at our core.' This is a doctrinal, on-the-record AdCom statement, not consultant interpretation.

findings.claim: Stern evaluates applicants across three lenses it calls 'the Three Cs': how they would do in the Classroom, how successful they'd be in their Career, and what kind of Character they have — and Gallogly singles out Character/EQ as the differentiator people underweight.
findings.evidence: Poets&Quants 'The MBA Gatekeeper at NYU Stern' (Aug 9, 2015) quotes Gallogly: the AdCom evaluates 'how well applicants would do in the classroom; how successful they would be in their careers; and what kind of character they have,' adding 'The piece that people don't talk about as much is the character.' He frames Character through IQ vs EQ — what sets Stern students apart is 'they understand how to interact with people, how to lead people.'

findings.claim: Stern defines the EQ it rewards as four competencies: self-awareness, empathy, communication, and self-management — and Loyd ties the 'Change' and 'Pick Six' essays directly to demonstrating these.
findings.evidence: Stern's EQ endorsement page: it values emotional intelligence competencies 'self-awareness, empathy, communication and self-management' as 'core' to its leadership community. Loyd (Clear Admit, June 2025) says the Change and Pick Six essays showcase 'your individual EQ — self-awareness, empathy, communication, resilience.'

findings.claim: The EQ Endorsement is a Stern-unique application component: a required character reference focused specifically on emotional intelligence, designed to surface traits applicants won't self-promote, and AdCom says it materially affects decisions in borderline cases.
findings.evidence: Gallogly (Stern news, March 7, 2020): 'Some people don't like to brag... there is something about someone else giving the example that gives more resonance to it. It definitely gave us some insight on people in ways... I don't think would have gotten in any other way, other than bringing the person in for an interview.' P&Q (2018) notes it 'provides another dimension and lens for the candidate — especially in borderline cases.' The primary endorser must be the current supervisor; an optional second can know the applicant professionally and/or personally; family members are excluded. Endorsers answer ~5 prompts, including comparing the applicant's performance to peers and giving 'one specific and compelling example to demonstrate the applicant's emotional intelligence.'

findings.claim: Self-awareness — specifically being willing to name a real weakness — is treated by Stern AdCom as a top signal of strength, not a liability.
findings.evidence: Gallogly (P&Q, Aug 2015): 'The biggest sign of strength is being OK saying what your weakness is. Self awareness is essential in the journey as a professional,' using a Superman analogy that flaws make candidates relatable and authentic. Loyd (June 2025) reinforces investing time reflecting on 'personal values, strengths, and development areas.'

findings.claim: Stern values insight over creativity in essays — it judges how insightful and authentic the content is, not how clever or polished it is.
findings.evidence: Gallogly (P&Q, Aug 2015): on essays, 'It's not about how creative it is, it's how insightful.' For Pick Six, multiple consultancies note AdCom's stated aim is to understand candidates 'as people' and reward authentic, themed, meaningful visuals over a random 'photo album.' Loyd: 'This is your chance as an applicant to tell your story' and 'Be authentic and be yourself!'

findings.claim: Stern runs genuine holistic review: every application is read in full by multiple reviewers across three dimensions — academic potential, professional achievements/aspirations, and alignment with Stern values.
findings.evidence: Loyd (Clear Admit, June 2025): 'every single application we receive is read in its entirety by multiple reviewers,' evaluated on academic potential, professional achievements/aspirations, and alignment with Stern values, with 'IQ + EQ' assessment throughout. Applications are read continuously (rolling) rather than batch-processed (Gallogly, P&Q 2018).

findings.claim: Interviews are invitation-only, required for admission, conducted ONLY by trained Stern admissions staff (not alumni/students) via Zoom, ~30 min, conversational and behavioral — probing self-awareness, communication style, and career fit. Roughly 20-30% of applicants are invited.
findings.evidence: Stern notification page: 'Interviews are available by invitation only,' 'conducted by our trained admissions professionals, who will have thoroughly read your entire application,' currently virtual; post-interview decision in up to ~3 weeks. Loyd (June 2025): 'by invitation only and is required for admission,' ~30% invited, 30 minutes, by admissions officers via Zoom, 'very conversational' to understand communication style and career fit. Leland/Fortuna (consultancies) estimate 20-25% invited and describe Stern's behavioral style probing self-awareness and interpersonal skills.

findings.claim: Pick Six is the AdCom's favorite component and the primary vehicle for assessing 'fit' as a person: six captioned images conveying values, motivations, perspective and personality beyond professional/academic achievement; AdCom rewards a unifying theme and authenticity, penalizes a random 'photo album.'
findings.evidence: Official essays page: Pick Six asks applicants to show 'qualities you will bring to the Stern community, beyond your professional and academic achievements' via a 3-sentence intro, six images, and one-sentence captions in a single PDF. Gallogly (P&Q 2018) on Pick Six: 'admissions meets Instagram'... 'gives you a very quick view and impression of an individual — what they value, what's important to them.' Consultancies (Stacy Blackman, Fortuna) report AdCom calls it their 'favorite part' and warn against a themeless photo album.

findings.claim: 'Fit' at Stern means a cohesive, authentic personal narrative aligned with Stern's values (IQ+EQ, collaboration, adaptability/'change'), plus concrete 'Why Stern / Why NYC' reasoning — not generic prestige-seeking.
findings.evidence: Loyd (June 2025): present 'a cohesive narrative of who you are, what your aspirations are,' reflecting on 'personal values, strengths, and development areas'; 'Be authentic and be yourself.' The 'Change: ___ it' essay (350 words, 2025-2026) is built on Stern's brand call to action and asks how the applicant views change and will 'live this at Stern,' explicitly testing values-fit. The goals essay (~500 words) requires 'Why is the Stern MBA the necessary next chapter... Please be specific.' Consultancies stress Why Stern/Why NYC clarity as a fit signal.

findings.claim: Explicit RED FLAGS / dealbreakers Stern describes: not reading instructions or email correspondence, trying to cut corners or 'not doing the work,' complaining about clearly-spelled-out requirements, vague/unspecific career goals, no clear Why Stern/Why NYC, no backup plan, surface-level answers, and poor judgment or inappropriate language in the interview.
findings.evidence: P&Q (2015) notes poor judgment in interviews — including inappropriate language — signals concern. Stacy Blackman's summary of Stern admissions guidance: 'a large number of applicants do not read application instructions or email correspondence,' and 'applicants who try to cut corners, do not do the work, or complain about something that was clearly spelled out... does not put a candidate in the best light.' Consultancies list lacking Why-Stern/Why-NYC clarity, no recruiting plan, and 'no backup plan' as red flags. Gallogly: surface-level answers 'don't show depth.' (Mix of one AdCom interview and consultant synthesis of Stern guidance — flagging the consultant-sourced items as medium.)

findings.claim: Authenticity is enforced as a hard rule: Stern will rescind an admission offer if the applicant did not write their own essays.
findings.evidence: Official Stern essays page guidance: 'an offer of admission will be rescinded if you did not write your essays.' This is a stated dealbreaker on plagiarism/ghostwriting (and, by extension, AI-generated essays).

findings.claim: GREEN FLAGS Stern describes: authenticity and being yourself; candid self-awareness about weaknesses; third-party (EQ-endorser) evidence of empathy/collaboration/leadership; insightful (not flashy) essays; specificity and a cohesive narrative; demonstrated leadership, teamwork, and communication; depth of knowledge in one's career field.
findings.evidence: Loyd (June 2025): 'Be authentic and be yourself'; cohesive narrative. Gallogly (2015/2018): naming your weakness is 'the biggest sign of strength'; Stern seeks students 'capable in leadership, managing teams and having emotional intelligence'; essays judged on insight; applicants should show depth and current knowledge of their field. The EQ endorsement is the AdCom's preferred channel for third-party evidence of these traits.

findings.claim: The two AdCom voices to cite are Isser Gallogly (Associate Dean of MBA Admissions & Program Innovation — architect of the EQ-focused application) and Lindsay Loyd (Assistant Dean of MBA Admissions — current operational head, most recent on-record statements June 2025). 'Apply as early as you are most competitive' is Gallogly's stated timing advice given rolling review.
findings.evidence: Stern news pages title Gallogly 'Associate Dean of MBA Admissions and Program Innovation.' Clear Admit (June 19, 2025) titles Loyd 'Assistant Dean of MBA Admissions' (her LinkedIn elsewhere lists 'Executive Director, MBA Admissions' — title nomenclature varies across sources). Gallogly (P&Q 2018): 'Apply as early as you are most competitive,' since Stern reads applications continuously rather than in deadline batches.

predictorImplications: Predictor weight: EQ signals are co-equal to academic/IQ signals at Stern, not a tiebreaker. Any model of Stern admit probability should add an explicit EQ feature set (evidence of empathy, collaboration, communication, self-awareness) and not treat GMAT/GPA + work pedigree as the dominant predictors as one might at more quant-weighted peers. The EQ Endorsement is the channel where this is most decisive in borderline cases.

The strongest controllable green-flag predictor is candid self-awareness: an essay/interview that names a genuine weakness and shows growth is treated by AdCom as a strength signal. A predictor should reward presence of an authentic weakness/growth narrative rather than penalize it.

'Fit' is operationalized and scorable at Stern via three concrete artifacts: the 'Change: ___ it' brand essay (values alignment), the Pick Six (personality/values/authenticity), and Why-Stern/Why-NYC specificity in the goals essay. A predictor can use presence/quality of each as discrete fit features rather than a vague 'fit' construct.

Process-discipline is a real penalty axis: not following instructions, cutting corners, vague goals, no backup plan, or inappropriate interview conduct are described as dealbreakers. These are detectable negative features (e.g., generic/Why-Stern-absent essays, instruction violations) and should carry meaningful downweight.

Authenticity is a hard gate: ghostwritten/AI-written essays trigger rescission. A predictor (and any applicant-advising tool) should treat self-authored, voice-consistent essays as a pass/fail gate, not just a quality slider.

Interview is high-signal and AdCom-controlled (staff-conducted, behavioral, ~20-30% invited, required for admission). An interview invite is itself a strong positive intermediate predictor of admission, and interview performance on self-awareness/communication should be modeled as a distinct, heavily weighted stage rather than folded into 'essays.'

Leadership continuity reduces cycle-to-cycle drift risk: the IQ+EQ doctrine, Three Cs, EQ endorsement, and Pick Six have been stable from Gallogly's 2017-2018 redesign through Loyd's June 2025 statements. A predictor can reasonably treat these criteria as stable across the Class of 2026 and Class of 2027 cycles.
findings.benchmarks: Cycle labeling for stats referenced: Interview invite rate ~30% of applicants is from Lindsay Loyd's Clear Admit Q&A dated June 19, 2025 (forward-looking guidance for the 2025-2026 application cycle, i.e., the incoming Class of 2028; not a Class of 2026 or 2027 figure). Independent consultancy estimates (Leland, Fortuna) put the interview-invite rate at ~20-25% — undated/rolling, treat as approximate. The essay set referenced (Change: ___ it at 350 words; 500-word goals/Why-Stern; Pick Six; optional 500-word additional info) is the officially published 2025-2026 cycle set per Stern's essays page and confirmed across 2025-2026 consultancy guides. Gallogly's quotes are time-stamped: Three Cs / weakness-as-strength / insight-over-creativity from Aug 9, 2015; EQ application redesign July 2017 and June/July 2018; EQ-endorsement-value reflection March 7, 2020. No hard admit-rate, GMAT, or class-profile NUMBERS were collected for this dimension (it is about philosophy/process), so none are asserted; any Class of 2026 vs Class of 2027 numeric class-profile comparison is OUT OF SCOPE here and should be sourced separately and labeled by graduating class. Confidence is LOW on any precise interview-invite percentage because the official ~30% (Loyd) and consultancy ~20-25% figures disagree and only the Loyd figure is from Stern itself.
redFlags: Title nomenclature for the current AdCom head is inconsistent across sources: Clear Admit (June 2025) calls Lindsay Loyd 'Assistant Dean of MBA Admissions' while her LinkedIn elsewhere shows 'Executive Director, MBA Admissions.' I used Clear Admit's title but flag the discrepancy — verify against Stern's own staff page before publishing a definitive title.

Several 'red flag' specifics (not reading instructions, cutting corners, no backup plan, Why-NYC clarity) come from named consultancies (Stacy Blackman, Leland, Fortuna) paraphrasing Stern guidance rather than a direct AdCom quote. They are credible and consistent but are second-hand; treat as medium confidence vs. the high-confidence doctrinal items quoted directly from Stern/Gallogly/Loyd.
The interview-invite rate is uncertain: Loyd says ~30% (official, 2025-2026 guidance) but consultancies say ~20-25%. Do not state a single precise number without caveat.

WebFetch summaries paraphrased some quotes; while I preserved quotation marks only around text the source rendered as direct quotes, exact wording of a few Gallogly/Loyd lines should be re-verified verbatim against the original article before being published as a direct quotation.

No on-the-record AdCom statement was found explicitly addressing AI-written essays; the 'rescission if you didn't write your essays' rule (official) is the closest dealbreaker and is being interpreted to cover AI/ghostwriting — that extension is reasonable but inferential.

This dimension is about philosophy/process, not class numbers. I deliberately did NOT collect or assert Class of 2026 vs Class of 2027 GMAT/GPA/acceptance-rate figures; if the parent report needs those, they must be sourced separately and labeled by graduating class to avoid mixing cycles.

checks.claim: Stern's central doctrine is 'IQ + EQ' — it weighs emotional/interpersonal intelligence as a co-equal core value, used to build the class. Loyd: 'We strive to build a class and community of individuals who demonstrate IQ + EQ.' Gallogly: 'IQ plus EQ is at our core.'
checks.corrected: Verbatim confirmed. Stern EQ endorsement page states 'IQ+EQ is a core value of NYU Stern' and seeks individuals with 'both intellectual and interpersonal strengths.' Clear Admit (June 2025) Loyd: 'Our emphasis on both intellectual intelligence and emotional intelligence, what we call IQ + EQ, is top of mind throughout the reading and evaluation process' and 'We strive to build a class and community of individuals who demonstrate IQ + EQ – this is core to our culture, and recruiters seek these qualities.' Gallogly (P&Q, June 11, 2018): 'IQ plus EQ is at our core.'

checks.claim: Stern evaluates across 'the Three Cs' — classroom, career, character — and Gallogly singles out Character as underweighted. Quotes from P&Q Aug 9, 2015.
checks.corrected: Verbatim confirmed (article dated August 9, 2015). Gallogly: applicants evaluated on 'how well applicants would do in the classroom; how successful they would be in their careers; and what kind of character they have'; 'The piece that people don't talk about as much is the character'; students 'understand how to interact with people, how to lead people, that they have superior communications skills.' Note: 'Three Cs' is the draft's/consultant shorthand label; Gallogly states the three dimensions but the source quoted does not use the literal trademark phrase 'the Three Cs.'

checks.claim: Stern defines the EQ it rewards as four competencies: self-awareness, empathy, communication, and self-management; Loyd ties Change and Pick Six essays to demonstrating these.
checks.corrected: EQ endorsement page lists exactly 'self-awareness, empathy, communication and self-management' as core EQ competencies — confirmed verbatim. Loyd (Clear Admit, June 2025) frames the essays' EQ as 'self-awareness, empathy, communication, resilience, openness to new ideas and diverse perspectives, etc.' — note her list says 'resilience' (plus 'openness to new ideas') rather than 'self-management,' so the official four-competency list and Loyd's essay-context list are worded slightly differently. CYCLE CAVEAT: Loyd ties this to the 'Change' essay, which existed in the 2025-2026 cycle but is NOT in the current (2026-2027) essay set (see essay-set check).

checks.claim: The EQ Endorsement is a required character reference focused on emotional intelligence; primary endorser must be current supervisor; optional second may know applicant professionally and/or personally; family excluded; endorsers answer ~5 prompts including comparing applicant to peers and giving a specific example of emotional intelligence. AdCom says it matters in borderline cases.
checks.corrected: Confirmed. One EQ endorsement required, optional second allowed; primary must be current supervisor (with explanation if not possible); 'Immediate family members should not write your EQ endorsement'; second endorser may know applicant professionally and/or personally. Endorsers answer five questions, including comparing the applicant to 'other well-qualified individuals in similar roles' and giving 'one specific and compelling example to demonstrate the applicant's emotional intelligence.' Gallogly (Stern news, March 7, 2020) confirmed verbatim: 'Some people don't like to brag... There is something about someone else giving the example that gives more resonance to it. It definitely gave us some insight on people in ways that we hadn't gotten before and I don't think would have gotten in any other way, other than bringing the person in for an interview.' P&Q 2018: endorsement 'provides another dimension and lens for the candidate — especially in borderline cases.'

checks.claim: No other top school has a required EQ endorsement — it is Stern-unique.
checks.corrected: Stern's EQ Endorsement is distinctive and widely described by consultancies as unique, and no authoritative source contradicts it, but I found no Stern or independent source that exhaustively verifies 'no other top school has this.' Treat the uniqueness claim as plausible-but-unverified rather than confirmed. The existence and mechanics of the EQ endorsement itself are fully confirmed.

checks.claim: Self-awareness — willing to name a real weakness — is treated as a top strength signal. Gallogly: 'The biggest sign of strength is being OK saying what your weakness is. Self awareness is essential...' (Superman analogy).
checks.corrected: Verbatim confirmed (P&Q Aug 9, 2015): 'The biggest sign of strength is being OK saying what your weakness is' and 'Self awareness is essential in the journey as a professional.' Superman/Kryptonite analogy confirmed. Loyd (June 2025) reinforces reflecting on 'personal values, strengths, and development areas.'

checks.claim: Stern values insight over creativity in essays: 'It's not about how creative it is, it's how insightful.'
checks.corrected: Verbatim confirmed (P&Q Aug 9, 2015): 'It's not about how creative it is, it's how insightful.' Loyd (June 2025) authenticity framing confirmed: 'This is your chance as an applicant to tell your story... The key word across all of that is YOU. Be authentic and be yourself!'

checks.claim: Genuine holistic review: every application is read in its entirety by multiple reviewers across three dimensions — academic potential, professional achievements/aspirations, alignment with Stern values.
checks.corrected: Verbatim confirmed. Loyd (June 2025): 'Every single application we receive is read in its entirety by multiple reviewers on the MBA Admissions team and evaluated holistically across three dimensions: academic potential, professional achievements and aspirations, and alignment with Stern core values.' Rolling/continuous review confirmed by Gallogly (P&Q 2018) and Stern's deadlines page ('reviews applications on an ongoing basis').

checks.claim: Interviews are invitation-only, required, conducted ONLY by trained Stern admissions staff (not alumni/students) via Zoom, ~30 min, conversational/behavioral; ~30% (Loyd) invited; consultancies estimate 20-25%; decision in ~3 weeks.
checks.corrected: Confirmed. Stern notification page: 'Interviews are available by invitation only'; 'conducted by our trained admissions professionals, who will have thoroughly read your entire application'; 'At this time, all interviews are being conducted virtually'; 'it will typically take up to three weeks to receive a post-interview decision.' Loyd (June 2025) verbatim: 'The interview is by invitation only and is required for admission. About 30 percent of applicants are invited to interview. Our interviews are 30 minutes long and conducted exclusively by admissions officers. All interviews are conducted via Zoom.' CAVEAT: the precise invite RATE is uncertain — Stern's own figure is ~30% (2025-2026 guidance, incoming Class of 2028); consultancy estimates of ~20-25% are undated/rolling. Do not state a single precise number without caveat (confidence on the exact percentage is low; on the structural facts, high).

checks.claim: Pick Six: a brief 3-sentence intro, six images, one-sentence captions, single PDF; conveys values/personality beyond professional/academic achievement; AdCom's 'favorite,' rewards unifying theme, penalizes random 'photo album.' Gallogly: 'admissions meets Instagram.'
checks.corrected: Structural requirements confirmed verbatim on Stern essays page: intro 'no more than 3 sentences,' six images illustrating 'interests, values, motivations, perspective and/or personality,' 'a one-sentence caption for each,' uploaded as a single PDF. Gallogly (P&Q 2018) verbatim: 'admissions meets Instagram,' giving 'a very quick view and impression of an individual — what they value, what's important to them.' The 'favorite component' and 'avoid a photo album' framing is consultant-sourced (Stacy Blackman, Fortuna) — credible but not a direct AdCom quote, so medium confidence on the 'favorite' superlative specifically. Pick Six IS in the current 2026-2027 set (confirmed live).

checks.claim: CURRENT ESSAY SET: 150-word short answer + 'Change: ___ it' essay (350 words) + 500-word goals/Why-Stern essay + Pick Six + optional 500-word additional info, described as 'the officially published 2025-2026 cycle set' and treated as stable across Class of 2026/2027.
checks.corrected: PARTLY STALE / CYCLE-MISLABELED. The 'Change: ___ it' (350w) essay and a 150-word professional-aspirations short answer were part of the 2025-2026 cycle (incoming Class of 2028). However, the CURRENT live Stern essays page (fetched June 2026 = the 2026-2027 cycle, incoming Class of 2029) shows NO 'Change' essay and NO 150-word short answer. The current set is: Essay 1 'Professional Aspirations' (500 words: 'What are your short-term career goals? Why is the Stern MBA the necessary next chapter in your professional story? Please be specific.'), Essay 2 Pick Six, and Essay 3 Additional Information (optional, 500 words). Clear Admit's essay analysis is now labeled 2026-2027 and likewise omits the Change essay. CORRECTION TO THE DRAFT: do not present 'Change: ___ it / 350 words' as a current or 'stable' feature — it was a 2025-2026 component that has been removed for 2026-2027. The draft's claim that the application has been 'stable from Gallogly's 2017-2018 redesign through June 2025' is wrong on this point: the 2018 set already had a single 500-word goals essay (no Change essay), the Change essay appeared later, and it is gone again in 2026-2027. The 'Why Stern' language is confirmed ('Why is the Stern MBA the necessary next chapter...'); however there is NO explicit 'Why NYC' language in the current goals-essay prompt — the NYC emphasis comes from Loyd's commentary and consultancies, not the prompt text.

checks.claim: Authenticity is a hard rule: 'an offer of admission will be rescinded if you did not write your essays.' (Extended by the draft to cover AI-written essays.)
checks.corrected: Verbatim confirmed on Stern essays page: 'An offer of admission will be rescinded if you did not write your essays.' The draft's extension of this rule to AI-generated essays is inferential — no on-the-record AdCom statement specifically naming AI was found. The rescission rule (ghostwriting/not authoring your own essays) is the closest stated dealbreaker; flag the AI extension as reasonable inference, not a quoted policy.

checks.claim: Red flags Stern describes: not reading instructions/email, cutting corners or 'not doing the work,' complaining about clearly-spelled-out requirements, vague goals, no Why-Stern/Why-NYC, no backup plan, surface-level answers, poor judgment/inappropriate language in interview.
checks.corrected: Mixed sourcing, as the draft flags. Direct AdCom: Gallogly (P&Q 2015) confirms poor judgment / using profanity (the 'F-word') in interviews signals concern and 'happens more often than one might think.' The 'not reading instructions / cutting corners / complaining about clearly-spelled-out requirements' items are paraphrases of Stern guidance via Stacy Blackman (consultant synthesis), not a verbatim AdCom quote. 'No backup plan (Plan B)' as a self-awareness/adaptability signal is confirmed in Leland's interview guide. 'Why NYC clarity' as a red flag is consultant framing (Leland frames NYC engagement as a fit point, not explicitly as a red flag). Treat the consultant-sourced specifics as medium confidence vs. the high-confidence Gallogly interview-conduct point.

checks.claim: Gallogly is 'Associate Dean of MBA Admissions and Program Innovation' (architect of the EQ application); Loyd is 'Assistant Dean of MBA Admissions' (current operational head, June 2025). 'Apply as early as you are most competitive' is Gallogly's timing advice.
checks.corrected: Gallogly title CONFIRMED: Stern news page (March 7, 2020) lists 'Associate Dean of MBA Admissions and Program Innovation.' 'Apply as early as you are most competitive' CONFIRMED verbatim (Gallogly, P&Q June 11, 2018), tied to rolling/continuous review. Loyd title CORRECTION: NYU Stern's OWN news page (dated June 20, 2025) titles her 'Executive Director for MBA Admissions,' and her LinkedIn also shows 'Executive Director, MBA Admissions.' Clear Admit's article calls her 'Assistant Dean of MBA Admissions.' The draft chose 'Assistant Dean' as the primary label — defer instead to Stern's own authoritative title: 'Executive Director for MBA Admissions' (note 'for,' not 'of'). She joined Stern in 2023 and leads Full-time MBA recruiting/admissions. Flag 'Assistant Dean' as a single-source (Clear Admit) variant that conflicts with Stern's own page.

checks.claim: Green flags: authenticity/be yourself; candid self-awareness about weaknesses; third-party EQ-endorser evidence of empathy/collaboration/leadership; insightful (not flashy) essays; specificity and cohesive narrative; demonstrated leadership/teamwork/communication; depth in one's field.
checks.corrected: Confirmed via the underlying verbatim quotes already validated (Loyd 'Be authentic and be yourself' / cohesive narrative / reflect on values, strengths, development areas; Gallogly weakness-as-strength and insight-over-creativity; EQ endorsement as the third-party channel). This is a reasonable synthesis of confirmed source material.

checks.claim: FT-vs-part-time test-waiver distinction (instruction-mandated check; draft does not itself assert this).
checks.corrected: NOT ASSERTED IN THE DRAFT (out of scope for the philosophy dimension), but verified for completeness per instructions: Stern's Full-time MBA and Part-time (Langone) MBA have SEPARATE test-waiver pathways and separate request forms. The Full-time program requires GMAT/GMAT Focus/GRE/EA/LSAT/MCAT/DAT unless a waiver is granted; the Part-time program offers, e.g., a Business Major Test Waiver (4-year US business degree, GPA >= 3.20). The Andre Koo Tech MBA is described as test-optional. If the parent report makes any test-waiver claim, it must specify the program — a FT waiver criterion is NOT interchangeable with a part-time one. (Exact 2026-2027 waiver windows shift each cycle; verify on Stern's standardized-tests pages before publishing specifics.)

checks.claim: 2026-2027 full-time MBA deadlines (context — draft did not assert these, marked class numbers out of scope).
checks.corrected: NOT ASSERTED IN THE DRAFT. Consultancy aggregators (Clear Admit, Stacy Blackman) list 2026-2027 full-time rounds as roughly R1 Sep 15, 2026 / R2 Oct 15, 2026 / R3 Jan 15, 2027 / R4 Apr 15, 2027, but as of June 2026 some sources note Stern had not yet officially confirmed all 2026-2027 dates. Verify against Stern's own deadlines page before publishing any deadline. Included only as context since the parent report may need it; not part of the philosophy dimension and not a draft claim.

verify.netAssessment: The draft's DOCTRINAL CORE is solid and well-sourced. Every Stern-authored or AdCom-quoted claim about philosophy verified verbatim against primary sources: IQ+EQ as a core value (EQ endorsement page + Loyd June 2025 + Gallogly 2018), the three classroom/career/character dimensions and weakness-as-strength and insight-over-creativity (Gallogly P&Q Aug 9, 2015, all verbatim), the four EQ competencies (self-awareness, empathy, communication, self-management — EQ page), the EQ-endorsement mechanics and borderline-case value (Stern pages + Gallogly March 7 2020 + P&Q 2018), full holistic 'read in its entirety by multiple reviewers' across three dimensions (Loyd), the invitation-only/required/staff-conducted/Zoom/~30-min interview (Stern notification page + Loyd), Pick Six structure and 'admissions meets Instagram,' the essay-rescission rule, and 'Apply as early as you are most competitive.' Gallogly's title is correct.\n\nTHREE CORRECTIONS NEEDED. (1) ESSAY SET / CYCLE LABELING is the most important: the draft presents 'Change: ___ it (350 words)' plus a 150-word short answer as the current set and claims the application has been STABLE since 2017-2018. That is wrong. The live Stern essays page for the current 2026-2027 cycle (Class of 2029) has NO Change essay and NO 150-word short answer — only a single 500-word Professional Aspirations essay, Pick Six, and a 500-word optional essay. The Change essay was a 2025-2026 (Class of 2028) feature that has been REMOVED for 2026-2027, and the 2018 set already had a single 500-word goals essay. So the 'stable across cycles' narrative and the Change-essay-as-current-fit-artifact must be re-cast as cycle-specific. (2) LOYD'S TITLE: defer to Stern's own page, 'Executive Director for MBA Admissions' (June 20, 2025), not Clear Admit's 'Assistant Dean.' (3) 'Why NYC' is NOT in the goals-essay prompt text — that emphasis is commentary/consultant-sourced, not the prompt. Lower-confidence/inferential items the draft already flags hold up: the interview-invite rate is genuinely uncertain (~30% official vs ~20-25% consultant), the AI-essay extension of the rescission rule is inference not policy, and the 'EQ endorsement is unique among top schools' superlative is plausible but unverified. The consultant-sourced red flags are credible but second-hand (medium). Bottom line: trust the philosophy doctrine as written; fix the essay-set/cycle claims and the Loyd title before publishing.

findings.dimension: Top MBA admissions consultancies' Stern-specific strategy
findings.summary: Across mbaMission, Stacy Blackman (SBC), Fortuna, Menlo Coaching, Stratus, Vantage Point, and Accepted, the unifying thesis is that NYU Stern admissions is organized around one explicit core value — "IQ + EQ" — and that Stern's distinctive application mechanics (the Pick Six visual essay, the EQ Endorsement, and a sharply specific "Why Stern" career essay) are all instruments for surfacing emotional intelligence, self-awareness, and authentic personality alongside analytical horsepower. Consultancies converge on a clear archetype Stern favors: the multidimensional, self-aware, collaborative candidate with a credible, specific career plan rooted in Stern's NYC finance/consulting/tech pipelines — and they converge on the candidate Stern screens out: the one-note, prestige-chasing "résumé in six images" applicant whose "Why Stern" could be pasted onto any top-15 school. IMPORTANT CYCLE DISTINCTION the consultancies are split on: For the current 2026-2027 cycle (Class of 2029), Stern REMOVED the 350-word "Change: ___ it" essay and the standalone 150-word short answer, and replaced them with a single 500-word Professional Aspirations essay that now explicitly bundles "Why is the Stern MBA the necessary next chapter" (confirmed by the official Stern essays page, Clear Admit's 2026-2027 analysis, Menlo's June-2026-updated guide, and Personal MBA Coach). The "Change: ___ it" (350w) essay plus 150-word short answer was the 2024-2025 and 2025-2026 format (Class of 2027/2028), and much consultancy content — SBC's tips page, Stratus, Accepted, and Fortuna's strategy article — still describes that older structure, so any advice referencing a "Change essay" should be treated as prior-cycle unless dated 2026-2027. The Pick Six and the EQ Endorsement (one required, one optional) persist across all cycles.

findings.claim: Stern's stated core value is 'IQ + EQ' and every consultancy treats it as the organizing principle for positioning; Stern seeks 'students who best exemplify Stern's core value: IQ+EQ' with strong intellectual ability, superior interpersonal skills, and a desire to create value for business and society.
findings.evidence: SBC, Fortuna, Menlo, Stratus, Accepted, and Vantage Point all independently anchor their Stern advice on the IQ+EQ framing. SBC states Stern 'seeks students who exemplify its core value of IQ+EQ.' Menlo: 'Stern's essays reward personality and self-knowledge more than most M7 applications do.' The practical implication all give: do not present as a pure analytical achiever — explicitly evidence empathy, self-awareness, and interpersonal strength.

findings.claim: CURRENT 2026-2027 CYCLE (Class of 2029): Stern removed the 'Change: ___ it' essay and the 150-word short answer, replacing them with a single 500-word Professional Aspirations essay that bundles career goals AND 'Why Stern': 'What are your short-term career goals? Why is the Stern MBA the necessary next chapter in your professional story? Please be specific.' Required essays are now Professional Aspirations (500w) + Pick Six; Additional Information (500w) is optional.
findings.evidence: Confirmed by the official Stern essays page ('Required Essays: 3' listing Professional Aspirations 500w, Pick Six, optional Additional Info 500w; no Change essay) and Clear Admit's 2026-2027 analysis. Personal MBA Coach states explicitly Stern 'removed its change essay this year and instead focuses applicants on post-MBA goals.' Menlo's June-2026-updated guide flags the word 'necessary' as signaling Stern wants why THIS program is indispensable, not whether an MBA helps.

findings.claim: PRIOR CYCLE FORMAT (2024-2025 and 2025-2026, Class of 2027/2028): the application used a 150-word short answer ('What are your short-term career goals?') PLUS a separate 350-word 'Change: _____ it' essay where the applicant fills the blank with a verb (Stern's own brand call-to-action examples include Dare/Dream/Drive/Empower/Manifest) and explains why it resonates and how they'll live it at Stern. Much consultancy content still describes this structure.
findings.evidence: Stratus, SBC, Accepted, and Fortuna's strategy article all describe the 350-word Change essay and 150-word short answer (Accepted lists deadlines Sept 15 2025 / Oct 15 2025 / Jan 15 2026 / Apr 15 2026 and word limits Essay 1 = 350). SBC's 2025-2026 tips and Fortuna's piece advise: the chosen word is 'only the first step — you need to bring it to life through a personal story and connect it to how you'll show up at Stern,' and the school emphasizes 'leaders of change.' Treat any 'Change essay' advice as prior-cycle for a Class-of-2029 applicant.

findings.claim: The Pick Six visual essay is Stern's signature differentiator and the single most consequential element to get right; the dominant consultancy mistake-warning is treating it as a second résumé instead of revealing 'who you are when you're not at work.'
findings.evidence: Menlo: 'The trap is treating it as a second résumé... the prompt explicitly asks for qualities beyond your professional and academic achievements'; build 'a coherent portrait, not a scrapbook,' choose 4-5 core qualities first then pick one image per dimension. Stratus reports Stern's adcom calls Pick Six 'their favorite part of the application.' Mistakes flagged: six images all conveying the same trait; diplomas/trophies/office photos; over-polished/engineered submissions ('authenticity is the point'). mbaMission warns against starting with 'catchy, cool-sounding slogans to wow the committee' — work backward from an authentic idea.

findings.claim: For Pick Six, captions — not images — carry the strategic weight: every consultancy says the caption must explain WHY the image matters, not describe what it shows.
findings.evidence: Menlo: 'The picture supplies the image; the caption supplies the why.' Accepted quotes Stern Admissions Director Nedjee Corriolan: 'the why means more than the what.' Stratus: 'Write captions that add real information... describe the context of the image and why it's significant' rather than identifying the obvious. Stratus also advises a diversity test: 'Have I provided six distinct perspectives on who I am as a person?' and to mix personal and professional with personal in the lead.

findings.claim: The 'Why Stern' / Professional Aspirations essay must be hyper-specific and non-portable — generic 'Why Stern' reasoning that applies to any top-15/top-25 school is the most-cited career-essay failure mode.
findings.evidence: Menlo: every Stern reference must be something 'you couldn't paste onto another school's essay'; lead with a specific role/function/org type ('strategy role at a growth-stage fintech,' not 'leadership'); spend real words naming specific Stern courses, professors, clubs, experiential offerings that close your skills gap. Clear Admit (2026-2027): name target firms and skills, show 'how exactly you would contribute' with concrete examples, and note 'speaking with alumni or admissions events can give you keen ideas on opportunities at Stern.' Vantage Point's school guidance frames Stern as needing a 'Stern-specific narrative... why this particular program — not just a generic top-15 MBA.'

findings.claim: Stern requires an EQ Endorsement (one required, one optional) instead of a conventional praise-the-work-skills recommendation — consultancies treat it as a distinct strategic asset to be briefed and aligned with the essays, not an afterthought.
findings.evidence: Stern requires one EQ Endorsement (a second optional); the required one should come from a current supervisor (with an explanation permitted if that creates professional risk); immediate family may not write it. Oriel Admissions: it tests self-awareness, empathy, communication, and self-management via 'one specific and compelling example' (~250-300 words); choose someone who 'observed your EQ in action,' brief them on the four EQ pillars and a specific moment (conflict resolution, difficult conversation, empathy under pressure), and ensure it complements — not duplicates or contradicts — your professional recommender. Stern's own guidance: 'you won't get extra points for including a letter from an executive just because of their title.'

findings.claim: Consultancies describe the candidate Stern FAVORS as multidimensional, self-aware, collaborative, and intentional — and the one it SCREENS OUT as the one-note high-achiever or 'superhero.'
findings.evidence: Menlo: 'Self-knowledge and restraint beat impressive-looking accomplishments every time'; Stern admits 'pair a credible professional plan with a vivid, authentic sense of identity.' Fortuna's mbaMission-style framing: these schools 'don't want superheroes — they want self-aware, empathetic, introspective leaders who uplift communities and can articulate who they are.' Clear Admit: come across as 'sincere, collaborative, and excited about the prospect of joining the NYU community.' SBC: 'be genuine... tell them about the real you.'

findings.claim: Stern's NYC location and finance/consulting/tech placement strength are positioning levers consultancies tell applicants to use concretely — a credible plan should map to Stern's proven pipelines, and the 'Why now / Why Stern' should exploit the Manhattan ecosystem.
findings.evidence: Menlo: 'Finance, consulting, and tech placement are Stern's proven pipelines' and should be referenced; its program guide cites placement of roughly 37% consulting and 22% investment banking, with NYC offering 'unmatched networking' even relative to Columbia for some industries, plus strong entrepreneurship resources. Class profile (Menlo): ~737 GMAT average, 3.64 GPA, 28 average age, 5.1 years work experience, 45% women, 43% international (cycle not precisely dated — treat as recent class).

findings.claim: The optional Additional Information essay (500 words) should only be used to explain genuine context (gaps, GPA/test concerns, reapplication), framed forward-looking, not as an excuse or a place to add a fourth essay — unanimous across consultancies.
findings.evidence: Fortuna: address transcript gaps, test score concerns, or employment breaks 'with transparency and forward-looking plans — not excuses.' Accepted and Stratus: 'Don't duplicate existing application content' / only submit if your application requires clarification. This is consistent across the older and current cycle since the optional essay is unchanged at 500 words.

findings.claim: mbaMission's Insider's Guide positions Stern's culture around innovation programs and explicit EQ development (the Change: Studio and 'IQ + EQ' development initiatives), which gives applicants concrete, school-specific hooks for fit essays.
findings.evidence: From mbaMission's published descriptions of its Insider's Guide: the guide explores Stern's 'commitment to innovation, including its Change: Studio and IQ + EQ development programs which emphasize emotional intelligence,' alongside location, class size, curriculum, clubs, and alumni involvement. (Full Insider's Guide pages returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch; this is drawn from mbaMission's own page summaries and resource listings.)

predictorImplications: For a Class-of-2029 (2026-2027) Stern applicant, a predictor should weight the 500-word Professional Aspirations essay heavily on Stern-specificity and non-portability: does the 'Why Stern' name concrete courses/professors/clubs/experiential offerings and a precise post-MBA role tied to Stern's NYC finance/consulting/tech pipelines? Generic 'Why Stern' is the single strongest negative signal consultancies cite.

Pick Six quality is a high-information, Stern-unique predictor: penalize submissions that read as a second résumé (trophies, diplomas, office shots), that repeat one trait across six images, or that are over-polished; reward six distinct dimensions, personal-over-professional balance, and captions that explain 'why it matters' rather than describing the image.

EQ signal should be modeled as a first-class predictor at Stern, not a generic soft factor: look for explicit evidence of self-awareness, empathy, communication, and self-management across the Pick Six, the EQ Endorsement strategy, and the career essay's tone (sincere/collaborative). Stern's IQ+EQ framing means a strong-stats, low-EQ profile is a screen-out risk distinct from most peer schools.

Treat the EQ Endorsement as a measurable lever: a required endorsement from a current supervisor (or justified alternative), built on one specific EQ anecdote, aligned-but-not-duplicative with the professional recommender, and NOT a title-chasing pick — title-over-relationship is an explicit Stern-stated non-value.

Flag a cycle-version risk in any model or advice corpus: a large share of public consultancy content still describes the retired 350-word 'Change: ___ it' essay and 150-word short answer. A predictor ingesting that content for a current applicant would optimize for a prompt that no longer exists; cycle-tagging the prompt set (2025-2026 vs 2026-2027) is essential.

Fit-to-archetype matters: model Stern's favored profile as multidimensional/self-aware/collaborative with a credible, attainable short-term goal in its proven pipelines, and treat the 'one-note achiever / superhero' presentation as a screen-out — this is a consistent, school-specific differentiator, not generic MBA advice.
redFlags: Cycle confusion is the biggest hazard: many consultancy pages (Stacy Blackman, Stratus, Accepted, Fortuna's strategy article, and some Menlo snippets) still present the 350-word 'Change: ___ it' essay plus 150-word short answer, which is the 2024-2025/2025-2026 format. The current 2026-2027 (Class of 2029) application has REMOVED the Change essay and uses a single 500-word Professional Aspirations essay. Always cross-check against the official Stern essays page before relying on a 'Change essay' tip.

Direct WebFetch to mbaMission's blog/Insider's Guide pages and Fortuna's article pages returned HTTP 403; mbaMission and Fortuna findings here rely partly on search-result summaries and mbaMission's own resource-page summaries rather than full primary text. Fortuna's most detailed Stern-specific essay analysis could not be fully captured verbatim — confidence on Fortuna's granular essay mechanics is medium where not corroborated.

Vantage Point publishes Stern deadlines/prompts but its deepest Stern strategy appears to sit behind school-specific guides not fully retrievable here; the Vantage Point characterization (Stern-specific narrative, why-this-program) is corroborated by Menlo/Clear Admit but the standalone Vantage Point essay-mechanics detail is thin in public pages.

Class-profile numbers (737 GMAT, 3.64 GPA, 28 age, 45% women, 43% international, ~37% consulting / 22% IB placement) come from Menlo's program pages and are not precisely cycle-dated on the page; treat as a recent class snapshot, not a specific Class-of-2027-vs-2028 figure, and verify against Stern's official class profile before quoting.

Some essay 'rules' (e.g., short-term goals only, name a job title and industry) are stated by aggregators (Target Test Prep, Vantage Point summaries) and align with consultancy advice but should be confirmed against the current official prompt wording, which now emphasizes 'short-term career goals' plus 'necessary next chapter' rather than long-term goals.
findings.benchmarks: Cycle/prompt structure: 2026-2027 (Class of 2029) = Professional Aspirations 500w (career goals + why Stern 'necessary next chapter') + Pick Six (3-sentence intro + 6 images each with a 1-sentence caption) + optional Additional Information 500w; no Change essay (official Stern essays page; Clear Admit 2026-2027; Personal MBA Coach; Menlo updated June 2026). 2024-2025 & 2025-2026 (Class of 2027/2028) = 150w short answer (short-term goals) + 'Change: ___ it' 350w + Pick Six + optional 500w (Stratus, SBC, Accepted, Fortuna). EQ Endorsement across cycles: 1 required (current supervisor preferred), 1 optional; ~250-300 words; tests self-awareness, empathy, communication, self-management; immediate family barred; no title bonus (Stern EQ Endorsements page; Oriel). Indicative recent class profile (Menlo, undated): ~737 GMAT avg, 3.64 GPA, age 28, 5.1 yrs WE, 45% women, 43% international; placement ~37% consulting, ~22% investment banking, growing tech.

checks.claim: Stern's stated core value is 'IQ + EQ' — seeking students with strong intellectual ability, superior interpersonal skills, and a desire to create value for business and society; consultancies anchor positioning on it.
checks.corrected: IQ+EQ is confirmed as Stern's stated core value; Stern's own materials describe seeking individuals with both intellectual and interpersonal strengths and emotional-intelligence skills (self-awareness, empathy, communication, self-management). The consultancy convergence on this framing is well supported.

checks.claim: CURRENT 2026-2027 cycle: Stern removed the 'Change: ___ it' essay and 150-word short answer, replacing them with a single 500-word Professional Aspirations essay: 'What are your short-term career goals? Why is the Stern MBA the necessary next chapter in your professional story? Please be specific.' Required = Professional Aspirations (500w) + Pick Six; Additional Information (500w) optional.
checks.corrected: Confirmed verbatim on the official Stern essays page: Essay 1 Professional Aspirations (500w, exact prompt as quoted), Essay 2 Personal Expression/Pick Six (required), Essay 3 Additional Information (500w, optional). No 'Change' essay or 150-word short answer appears. Clear Admit's 2026-2027 analysis corroborates.

checks.claim: PRIOR cycle (2025-2026): 150-word short answer ('short-term career goals') PLUS a 350-word 'Change: ___ it' essay; Stern's verb examples included Dare/Dream/Drive/Empower/Manifest; prompt asked why the word resonates and how you'll embrace it at Stern.
checks.corrected: Confirmed for 2025-2026: 150w short answer ('What are your short-term career goals?') + 'Change: _____ it' (350w) with Stern examples Dare/Dream/Drive/Empower/Manifest, asking 'Why does this word resonate with you? How will you embrace your own personal tagline while at Stern?' Pick Six and optional 500w also present. Correctly retired for 2026-2027.

checks.claim: Pick Six rules: a 3-sentence (max) intro, six images, one one-sentence caption each; explicitly asks for qualities beyond professional/academic achievements; caption's 'why' matters more than the 'what.'
checks.corrected: Official page confirms: intro no more than 3 sentences, six images, one-sentence caption per image explaining why it was selected and is significant. Captions ask interests/values/motivations/perspective/personality. The 'why over what' framing is well supported by consultancy sources.

checks.claim: EQ Endorsement: one required, one optional; required one should come from current supervisor (with explanation permitted if risky); immediate family barred; no extra points for an executive's title; ~250-300 words testing self-awareness, empathy, communication, self-management.
checks.corrected: Confirmed on official page: 'Your EQ Endorsement must come from your current supervisor' (explain if unable); 'Immediate family members should not write your EQ endorsement'; one required, second optional (more than two allowed but not encouraged). The ~250-300 word figure and four EQ pillars come from Oriel, not the official page (official page gives no word limit). The 'no extra points for an executive's title' line is from the Stern Full-time MBA admissions BLOG, not the official EQ requirements page — draft's attribution to 'Stern's own guidance' is fair but the precise source is the blog.

checks.claim: Class profile cited from Menlo (treated as undated/recent): ~737 GMAT, 3.64 GPA, age 28, 5.1 yrs WE, 45% women, 43% international.
checks.corrected: All six numbers match the official Stern Full-Time MBA Class Profile, and this is specifically the CLASS OF 2027 (enrolled fall 2025) — not an undated snapshot. Exact: GMAT 737 (10th Ed.; GMAT Focus avg 682), GPA 3.64, age 28, 5.1 yrs WE, 45% women, 43% international (43 countries), 4,933 applications, 336 enrolled. The draft's hedge ('undated, recent class snapshot') can be upgraded to 'Class of 2027, official.'

checks.claim: Placement: ~37% consulting and ~22% investment banking (from Menlo program page).
checks.corrected: These figures are unreliable as quoted. Menlo's own page is internally inconsistent: its intro text says '37% into consulting and over 22% into investment banking' (post-MBA placement) while its detailed employment table shows 32.8% consulting / 28.0% investment banking. Separately, the draft risks conflating PLACEMENT (outcomes) with PRE-MBA BACKGROUND. The official Class of 2027 PRE-MBA industry mix is Financial Services 31% / Consulting 15% (per Poets&Quants/Clear Admit) — a different metric. Bottom line: do not quote '37%/22%' as a fact; if citing placement, use Menlo's detailed-report figures (~33% consulting / ~28% IB) and flag they are not officially dated to a single class year, and never present pre-MBA background (31% finance / 15% consulting) as placement.

checks.claim: Optional Additional Information essay is 500 words and should explain genuine context (gaps, GPA/test concerns, reapplication), not add a fourth essay; unchanged across cycles.
checks.corrected: Confirmed: optional 500w Additional Information essay for hardships, employment gaps, undergraduate-record concerns, plans to retake tests, or other relevant information. Present at 500w in both 2025-2026 and 2026-2027 FT cycles.

checks.claim: mbaMission's Insider's Guide positions Stern's culture around Change: Studio and 'IQ + EQ' development programs.
checks.corrected: Confirmed. Change: Studio is a real Stern co-curricular program (post-orientation leadership/experiential/entrepreneurial programming to navigate change), and mbaMission's Insider's Guide explicitly references Change: Studio and 'IQ + EQ' development programs. Solid school-specific fit hook.

checks.claim: 2026-2027 full-time MBA deadlines: R1 Sept 15 2025, R2 Oct 15 2025, R3 Jan 15 2026, R4 Apr 15 2026 (as listed via Accepted's prior-cycle dates in the draft).
checks.corrected: The draft's deadline DATES (Sept 15 2025 / Oct 15 2025 / Jan 15 2026 / Apr 15 2026) are the 2025-2026 (prior) cycle and are attributed to Accepted as such. For the CURRENT 2026-2027 cycle the official dates are: R1 Sept 15, 2026 (notify Dec 1, 2026); R2 Oct 15, 2026 (notify Jan 1, 2027); R3 Jan 15, 2027 (notify Apr 1, 2027); R4 Apr 15, 2027. The month/day pattern repeats; only the year advances. Any model using a 2025/2026-dated deadline for a current applicant is one cycle stale.

checks.claim: Full-time MBA is effectively test-required (not test-optional); waiver distinction between FT and part-time matters (special-attention item).
checks.corrected: Confirmed and important: the Full-Time (and Luxury & Retail) MBA is NOT test-optional — 'A valid standardized test score is required for all MBA applicants to the Full-time MBA and Luxury & Retail MBA programs.' FT waivers are narrow: a general academic-readiness waiver plus the NYU+MBA waiver (NYU undergrad, cumulative GPA >=3.20). FT test-waiver REQUEST deadline for Fall 2027 is April 1, 2027 (a prior search snippet citing 'April 1, 2026' was the Fall-2026 cycle). By contrast, the PART-TIME (Langone) MBA offers a much broader waiver menu: Business-major (GPA >=3.20), Engineering-major (committee approval), NYU-undergrad (GPA >=3.20), and U.S. military/veterans. The Andre Koo Tech MBA is separately test-optional. Any advice implying the FT MBA is broadly test-optional/waiver-friendly is wrong; the generous waivers are a part-time feature.

checks.claim: Current 2026-2027 applicants are the Class of 2029; the prior 'Change essay' format applied to Class of 2027/2028.
checks.corrected: Class-year labeling is correct for a 2-year MBA: 2026-2027 cycle applicants enroll fall 2027 and graduate spring 2029 = Class of 2029. The most recent ENROLLED class (the official profile above) is the Class of 2027. The 2025-2026 'Change essay' cycle produced the Class of 2028. Draft's cycle-to-class mapping is sound.

checks.claim: Consultancy 'Change essay' content is prior-cycle FT material; it is not a current part-time prompt either.
checks.corrected: Confirmed and worth adding to the draft: the part-time (Langone) MBA does NOT use a 'Change' essay. Its current essays are Professional Aspirations (350w; short- AND long-term goals + how the Part-time MBA helps), 'Person, Place & Thing' (three images, up to 150 words each), and an optional 250w. So 'Change essay' advice is unambiguously a retired FULL-TIME prompt — not a live part-time prompt — which strengthens the draft's cycle-confusion red flag.

verify.netAssessment:
The draft's central thesis and most hard claims hold up well against authoritative sources. CONFIRMED with high confidence: (1) the IQ+EQ core-value framing; (2) the current 2026-2027 essay structure — Professional Aspirations 500w (exact prompt verbatim) + Pick Six + optional 500w Additional Information, with the 'Change: ___ it' essay and 150-word short answer removed (official Stern essays page + Clear Admit); (3) the prior 2025-2026 format including the exact Dare/Dream/Drive/Empower/Manifest examples; (4) Pick Six mechanics (3-sentence intro, six images, one-sentence captions); (5) the EQ Endorsement rules (one required from current supervisor, family barred, no title bonus); and (6) the Class of 2027 profile numbers, which I can UPGRADE from the draft's 'undated/recent' hedge to officially being the Class of 2027 (GMAT 737, GPA 3.64, age 28, 5.1 yrs WE, 45% women, 43% international, 4,933 apps, 336 enrolled).

TWO corrections and one strengthening are warranted. CORRECTION 1 (medium confidence, the draft already flagged it): the '37% consulting / 22% IB' placement figures are unreliable — Menlo's own page is internally inconsistent (its detailed report shows ~33% consulting / ~28% IB), and the draft risks conflating post-MBA placement with pre-MBA background (the official Class-of-2027 pre-MBA mix is Financial Services 31% / Consulting 15%, a different metric). Do not quote 37%/22% as fact. CORRECTION 2 (high confidence): for the CURRENT cycle the deadlines are R1 Sept 15 2026 / R2 Oct 15 2026 / R3 Jan 15 2027 / R4 Apr 15 2027 — the draft surfaces the 2025-2026 dates (correctly tagged to Accepter/prior cycle, but a model must not use them for a current applicant).

SPECIAL-ATTENTION FT-vs-part-time test-waiver distinction: now fully resolved and a genuine, high-value finding. The Full-Time MBA is NOT test-optional (test required for all FT applicants; only a narrow academic-readiness waiver and the NYU+MBA GPA-3.20 waiver; FT waiver-request deadline April 1, 2027). The broad, generous waiver menu (business major, engineering major, NYU undergrad, military/veteran) belongs to the PART-TIME Langone MBA. The draft does not make this explicit and should add it. Relatedly, the part-time program does NOT use a 'Change' essay (it uses Professional Aspirations 350w + 'Person, Place & Thing' + optional 250w), which strengthens the draft's cycle-confusion red flag: 'Change essay' content is a retired FULL-TIME prompt, not a live part-time one. Net: the draft is substantially accurate and well-cycle-tagged; tighten the placement statistics, advance the deadline years, and add the FT-vs-part-time test-required/waiver distinction.

findings.dimension: NYU Stern interview + decision intelligence (Clear Admit and official sources)
findings.summary: NYU Stern's MBA interview is non-blind (resume/application-based), invitation-only, required for admission, conducted virtually over Zoom by a trained admissions-committee member, and runs roughly 25-40 minutes. It is highly conversational and tailored to the candidate. The most reliably reported question set across Clear Admit interview reports is: a question about the applicant's "Pick Six" image slides; the classic "Why MBA / Why Stern / Why now"; one or two resume-walkthrough questions; one or two behavioral ("tell me about a time") questions including a problem-solving/challenge prompt; short-term goals plus a contingency/Plan B; internship and recruiting strategy; strengths/weaknesses; and interests outside work, ending with ~10 minutes for the candidate's own questions. Per Stern's official notification page, post-interview decisions typically take up to three weeks. Decisions are released round-by-round (R1 by Dec 1, R2 by Jan 1, R3 by Apr 1, R4 ongoing for the 2026-27 cycle), with rolling/ongoing notifications beginning shortly after each deadline; candidates can be waitlisted or denied without an interview. The waitlist is explicitly NOT ranked, with no predetermined size; no individual feedback is given; applicants self-improve via the Waitlist Update Form (retake GMAT/GRE/EA — highest score counts, new coursework/credentials like CFA/CPA, updated resume, supplemental recommendation letter emailed to admchair@stern.nyu.edu, and a brief continued-interest essay). Waitlist review begins in spring and can run until late August orientation. Clear Admit LiveWire data points (Class of 2027 / 2026 cycle) show waitlist admits as late as May (some via phone) and a wide stat spread (e.g., GMAT 700/GPA 2.94 WL-admit; GRE 316 WL-admit; GMAT 615/GPA 3.74 R4 denial; EA 154 R2 WL-reject).

findings.claim: The Stern interview is non-blind (resume/application-based) — the interviewer has read the full application before the meeting.
findings.evidence: Stern's official process page and multiple Clear Admit summaries state interviews are application-based and the interviewer reviews the complete application first; candidates get specific follow-ups on resume, Pick Six images, and stated goals. This is the opposite of a blind/resume-only interview.

findings.claim: Interviews are invitation-only, required for admission, and conducted virtually (Zoom) by a trained admissions-committee member; ~25-40 minutes, conversational.
findings.evidence: Stern process page: 'Interviews are available by invitation only' and 'conducted virtually' by 'trained admissions professionals.' Clear Admit reports from this cycle list interviewer as Adcom and format as Zoom; durations reported at 25 min (R3), 30-40 min (R4).

findings.claim: Most frequently reported question set: a 'Pick Six' image question; 'Why MBA / Why Stern / Why now'; one or two resume-walkthrough questions; one or two behavioral/'tell me about a time' questions (often a problem-solving/challenge); short-term goal + contingency plan; internship/recruiting strategy; strengths & weaknesses; interests outside work; then candidate Q&A.
findings.evidence: Three current-cycle Clear Admit reports converge on this list. R3 (Apr 4): cycling hobby opener, 'Why MBA?', 'Why now?', short-term goal + contingency, 'TMAT proud at work', recent accomplishments, 'What do you do outside work?'. R3 (Apr 1): 'Pick 6' question, resume overview, 'TMAT problem solving/challenge at work', strengths/weaknesses, MBA motivation, internship/career plans, 'Why Stern?', outside interests. R4 (Apr 2): one Pick Six question, 'Why MBA/Stern/now', 1-2 resume questions, ~2 behavioral, internship/post-grad role plans, ~10 min for candidate questions.

findings.claim: Post-interview decisions typically take up to three weeks.
findings.evidence: Stern's official notification page: 'After the interview, it will typically take up to three weeks to receive a post-interview decision.' Post-interview outcomes are admit, waitlist, or denial.

findings.claim: Round-by-round notification: notifications begin shortly after each deadline and continue rolling; candidates can be waitlisted or denied WITHOUT an interview.
findings.evidence: Process page: 'Initial notifications will begin shortly after the first application deadline' and continue on an ongoing basis; initial outcomes are interview invite, waitlist, or denial, and candidates 'may be placed on the waitlist or denied admission without being interviewed.'

findings.claim: 2026-27 cycle (Class of 2028) deadlines/decisions: R1 deadline Sep 15, 2026 / decision by Dec 1, 2026; R2 Oct 15, 2026 / Jan 1, 2027; R3 Jan 15, 2027 / Apr 1, 2027; R4 Apr 15, 2027 / ongoing.
findings.evidence: Clear Admit's Stern deadlines page lists these four rounds with paired decision-notification dates for the 2026-27 season (entering Class of 2028). R4 decisions are 'ongoing/rolling.'

findings.claim: The Stern waitlist is explicitly NOT ranked, has no predetermined size, and Stern gives no individual application feedback.
findings.evidence: Official Waitlist FAQ: 'The waitlist is not ranked and the number of applicants on the waitlist varies... There is no pre-determined number.' On feedback: 'We do not provide specific feedback on individual applications.' Number admitted off the waitlist 'varies from year to year.'

findings.claim: Waitlisted applicants self-improve via the Waitlist Update Form: retake GMAT/GRE/EA (highest overall score counts), submit new coursework/credentials (CFA, CPA), updated resume, a supplemental recommendation letter (emailed to admchair@stern.nyu.edu), and a brief continued-interest essay. Visiting campus does not help; demonstrate interest via supplemental info.
findings.evidence: Official Waitlist FAQ details all of these update channels. It states 'We consider only your highest overall score(s), so there is no downside to retaking,' and that the best way to demonstrate interest is uploading supplemental info via the status page or Waitlist Update Form (not campus visits). Adcom 'reserves the right to make a final decision prior to receiving additional material.'

findings.claim: Waitlist review begins in spring and can continue until late August (orientation); status updates come by email to check on the application status page.
findings.evidence: Official Waitlist FAQ: 'the waitlist review process begins in the spring and can continue until the orientation... a final decision may not be made until late August' for the two-year program; 'You will receive an email when there has been an update to your status.'

findings.claim: Clear Admit LiveWire data points (Class of 2027 / 2026 cycle): waitlist admits occurred as late as mid-to-late May, some delivered by phone, with a wide stat spread; denials and waitlist-rejections also reported into June.
findings.evidence: LiveWire entries: R1 waitlist-ADMIT May 21, 2026 via phone, GMAT 700 / GPA 2.94 (declined for lack of $); R3 waitlist-ADMIT May 14, 2026 via phone, GRE 316, finance/IB; R4 DENIAL Jun 18, 2026, GMAT 615 / GPA 3.74, healthcare; R2 waitlist-REJECT Jun 22, 2026 (portal Jun 17), EA 154, Mexico City. Interview invites in R3-R4 included a GMAT 715 candidate and test-waiver candidates.

findings.claim: Historically Stern has used on-campus in-person interviews (older Clear Admit reports), but the current cycle is virtual/Zoom — note the format shift when reading older reports.
findings.evidence: A Clear Admit R1 'Adcom / On-Campus' report (pub. June 2015) describes an in-person interview with a customized packet and a role-play ('pretend I was interviewing for that company'). Current-cycle reports and the official page describe virtual interviews, so on-campus reports are dated context, not current format.

findings.claim: Class profile context for benchmarking applicant data points: Class of 2027 (entered fall 2025) avg GMAT 737 (10th edition; 80% 690-760), GMAT Focus avg 682, avg GPA 3.64 (80% 3.36-3.89), ~24% admit rate (4,933 apps / 1,161 admits / 336 enrolled), 45% women, 43% international.
findings.evidence: Clear Admit and Poets&Quants Class of 2027 profile coverage report these figures; admit rate ~24% derived from 1,161/4,933. Stern reports GMAT under both the 10th-edition (737 avg) and Focus (682 avg) scales.

findings.benchmarks: Stern interview-to-decision SLA (official): up to 3 weeks post-interview. Round decision dates (2026-27 cycle): R1 Dec 1, R2 Jan 1, R3 Apr 1, R4 ongoing. Reported interview lengths: 25 min (R3), 30-40 min (R4). Candidate stats from LiveWire (Class of 2027 cycle): waitlist-admit GMAT 700/GPA 2.94; waitlist-admit GRE 316; R4 denial GMAT 615/GPA 3.74; R2 waitlist-reject EA 154. Class of 2027 admit rate ~24%; avg GMAT 737 (10th ed.) / 682 (Focus); avg GPA 3.64.
predictorImplications: Interview-invite probability and a non-blind, resume-driven format mean a predictor should weight the consistency/depth of the applicant's resume narrative and Pick Six story highly — Stern interviewers probe specifics, so a thin or inconsistent application surfaces in interview and decision.

Model the post-interview decision window as up to 3 weeks (official SLA) and gate the round-level decision dates (R1 Dec 1, R2 Jan 1, R3 Apr 1) for timeline prediction; treat R4 and waitlist as open-ended/rolling.

Waitlist is unranked with admits arriving as late as May-August — a predictor should treat Stern waitlist outcomes as high-variance and time-extended, not a near-term binary; model 'continued interest + concrete updates (score retake, new credential, supplemental rec)' as the primary lever an applicant controls.

Wide LiveWire stat spread (e.g., GMAT 700/GPA 2.94 waitlist-admit vs GMAT 615/GPA 3.74 denial) confirms Stern's holistic, EQ/fit-weighted evaluation — a purely stats-driven admit predictor will misfire; soft factors (Why-now clarity, recruiting plan realism, community fit) carry real signal.

Distinguish cycles in any training data: pull interview-question priors from Class of 2027/2026-cycle reports (Zoom, Pick Six) and discard the on-campus/role-play format from pre-2020 reports as stale.
A 'Plan B / contingency goal' question is consistently asked — predictors scoring interview readiness should flag applicants whose post-MBA goal lacks a credible fallback.
redFlags: Clear Admit interview reports rarely include candidate GMAT/GPA/work background, so question-frequency claims are well-sourced but cannot be cross-tabbed against admit outcomes for the same individuals — admit/ding-vs-question correlations are not available.

Sample size is small: only ~3-4 current-cycle Stern interview reports and a handful of LiveWire entries were retrievable, so 'most frequent question' rankings are directional, not statistically robust.
LiveWire entries are self-reported and unverified; stats like GMAT 700/GPA 2.94 or EA 154 should be treated as anecdotal, hence medium confidence on that finding.

The Waitlist FAQ PDF used (apply.stern.nyu.edu/waitlistfaq.pdf vs an older 2022-2023 web-docs copy) is current but undated in-body; policy wording could shift cycle-to-cycle — re-verify against the live PDF each cycle.
GMAT reporting is split between two scales (10th-edition ~737 and Focus ~682); conflating them will distort any stats benchmark — always label which scale a data point uses.

Round decision dates are from Clear Admit's deadlines page (2026-27 cycle / Class of 2028); confirm against Stern's official deadlines page before relying on exact dates, as Stern occasionally adjusts round structure.

checks.claim: The Stern interview is non-blind: the interviewer reads the full application before the meeting (application-based, not a blind/resume-only interview).
checks.corrected: Official page wording: interviews are conducted by 'trained admissions professionals, who will have thoroughly read your entire application.' Coaching sources explicitly call it 'not a blind interview.' Confirmed. NOTE: the draft cites the notification page as '/programs-admissions/mba-programs/admissions/notification-process' — the live full-time URL is '/programs-admissions/full-time-mba/admissions/notification-process'. The /mba-programs/ path is a hub, not the FT notification page; fix the citation.

checks.claim: Interviews are invitation-only, conducted virtually (Zoom) by a trained admissions-committee member; required (you must be interviewed to be admitted), roughly 25-40 minutes, conversational.
checks.corrected: Official page: 'Interviews are available by invitation only' and 'At this time, all interviews are being conducted virtually,' by 'trained admissions professionals.' Clear Admit current-cycle reports show Adcom/Zoom with durations of 25 min (R3, Apr 1, 2026) and 30-40 min (R4, Apr 2, 2026); coaching sources cite ~30 min. The official page does NOT itself state a duration — the 25-40 min range is sourced from Clear Admit reports/coaching, which is fine, but should not be attributed to the official page. R3 (Apr 1, 2026): Pick Six, resume walkthrough, TMAT problem-solving/challenge, strengths/weaknesses, Why MBA, internship/career plans, Why Stern, outside interests, candidate Q&A. R4 (Apr 2, 2026): Pick Six, Why MBA/Stern/now, 1-2 resume questions, 2 behavioral. Sources note an explicit EQ emphasis. Directional (small n) but well-supported.

checks.corrected: Verbatim from the official page: 'After the interview, it will typically take up to three weeks to receive a post-interview decision.' Outcomes: offer of admission, waitlist, or denial. Confirmed.

checks.corrected: Official page: 'Initial notifications will begin shortly after the first application deadline and continue on an ongoing basis,' with three initial outcomes (interview invite, waitlist, denial), and 'you may be placed on the waitlist or denied admission without being interviewed.' Confirmed. Minor: the official page frames this as initial-vs-post-interview, not an explicit 'round-by-round' calendar — round decision DATES come from Clear Admit's deadlines page (next check), not this page.

checks.claim: 2026-27 cycle (Class of 2028) deadlines/decisions: R1 Sep 15, 2026 / by Dec 1, 2026; R2 Oct 15, 2026 / Jan 1, 2027; R3 Jan 15, 2027 / Apr 1, 2027; R4 Apr 15, 2027 / ongoing.

checks.corrected: Verified verbatim from the live FAQ PDF: 'The waitlist is not ranked and the number of applicants on the waitlist varies throughout the admissions cycle. There is no pre-determined number of waitlisted applicants.' On feedback: 'We do not provide specific feedback on individual applications.' Confirmed.

checks.claim: Waitlisted applicants self-improve via a 'Waitlist Update Form': retake GMAT/GRE/EA (highest overall score counts), new coursework/credentials (CFA/CPA), updated resume, a supplemental recommendation letter emailed to admchair@stern.nyu.edu, and a brief continued-interest essay.
checks.corrected: PARTIALLY OVER-ATTRIBUTED. Confirmed in the live FAQ: retake 'the GMAT or GRE' with 'We consider only your highest overall score(s), so there is no downside to retaking'; email scheduled test date and unofficial score to admchair@stern.nyu.edu; new credentials 'e.g. CFA, CPA, etc.'; updated resume; 'Submit a supplemental recommendation letter... emailed by the author to admchair@stern.nyu.edu.' CORRECTIONS: (a) The current FAQ does NOT use the phrase 'Waitlist Update Form' — it says upload via the 'application status page' and select 'Supplemental Information.' (b) The FAQ's retake passage names only 'GMAT or GRE,' NOT the Executive Assessment/EA — drop EA from this attribution. (c) There is NO mention of a 'continued-interest essay' as a named deliverable; the FAQ instead lists informing Stern of continued commitment / new community involvement and submitting supplemental info. The Adcom 'reserves the right to make a final decision on your application prior to receiving additional material' IS confirmed verbatim.

checks.corrected: FAQ (Q: 'Should I visit campus?'): 'Interviews are available by invitation only. The best way to demonstrate your interest is by uploading appropriate supplemental information via the application status page.' Confirmed.
