For example, for the two-year Full-time MBA program, a final decision may not be made until late August.' And: 'You will receive an email when there has been an update to your status.' Confirmed.

checks.claim: Clear Admit LiveWire data points (Class of 2027 / 2026 cycle): waitlist admits as late as mid-to-late May (some by phone) with a wide stat spread (GMAT 700/GPA 2.94 WL-admit; GRE 316 WL-admit; GMAT 615/GPA 3.74 R4 denial; EA 154 R2 WL-reject); denials/WL-rejects into June.
checks.corrected: NOT independently confirmable. LiveWire entries are self-reported, transient, and not durably addressable; the specific dated data points (e.g., May 21, 2026 WL-admit GMAT 700/GPA 2.94; EA 154 WL-reject) could not be re-located/verified at fact-check time. The draft already flags these as anecdotal/medium — they should be treated as illustrative only, NOT as benchmarks, and confidence is appropriately at-or-below medium (I'd put it at low for the exact figures). Directionally plausible (Stern WL admits do extend into spring/summer per the FAQ), but the individual stats are unverified.

checks.corrected: Supported: the official page states 'At this time, all interviews are being conducted virtually,' and all current-cycle Clear Admit reports show Zoom — so pre-2020 on-campus/role-play reports are dated context. Reasonable caveat; confirmed.

checks.claim: Class profile (Class of 2027, entered fall 2025): avg GMAT 737 (10th ed.; 80% 690-760), GMAT Focus avg 682 (80% 645-725), avg GPA 3.64 (80% 3.36-3.89), ~24% admit rate (4,933 apps / 1,161 admits / 336 enrolled), 45% women, 43% international.
checks.corrected: All figures match the OFFICIAL Stern class-profile page for the Class of 2027: 4,933 applications, 1,161 admits (=23.5%, rounds to ~24%), 336 enrolled; GMAT 10th-ed avg 737 (80% 690-760); GMAT Focus avg 682 (80% 645-725); GPA avg 3.64 (80% 3.36-3.89); GRE Q avg 164 / V avg 163; 45% women; 43% international; avg age 28; avg ~5.1 yrs work experience. Confirmed. One labeling caveat the draft already raises: always tag which GMAT scale (10th-ed 737 vs Focus 682) a benchmark uses.

checks.claim: [FOCUS AREA — FT vs part-time test-waiver distinction] The full-time MBA requires a standardized test but allows a waiver request (bachelor's required, 300-word essay, ~3-week decision); the part-time (Langone) MBA has separate, broader categorical waivers (3.20 GPA business/engineering/NYU-undergrad, military/veteran).
checks.corrected: NOT IN THE DRAFT — flagged gap. Verified: Full-Time MBA — 'A valid standardized test score is required for all MBA applicants to the Full-time MBA and Luxury & Retail MBA'; waiver requires a bachelor's already received, applying in the 2026-27 cycle, and a 300-word essay; Adcom decides 'within 3 weeks of submission' on a rolling basis. Part-Time (Langone) MBA — categorical waivers: business major OR engineering major (engineering needs Adcom written approval) with cumulative GPA >= 3.20 from an accredited U.S. school; NYU undergrad with GPA >= 3.20; and active U.S. military/veterans. The part-time waiver page lists NO certification-based (CPA/CFA) or years-of-experience waiver. The draft's claim set omits this FT-vs-PT waiver distinction entirely — it should be added, as it materially affects who must submit a test.

verify.netAssessment: Net: the draft is largely accurate and well-sourced on the high-stakes items, but has two material corrections and one flagged gap. CONFIRMED (high confidence): non-blind format; invitation-only virtual Zoom Adcom interviews; converging question set (Pick Six, Why MBA/Stern/now, resume, behavioral/TMAT, short-term goal + contingency, strengths/weaknesses, outside interests, EQ emphasis); 'up to three weeks' post-interview SLA; rolling notifications and waitlist/denial without interview; the full 2026-27 deadline grid (R1 Sep 15/Dec 1; R2 Oct 15/Jan 1; R3 Jan 15/Apr 1; R4 Apr 15/ongoing); waitlist not ranked / no predetermined size / no individual feedback / campus visits don't help / review begins spring and can run to late August; and ALL Class of 2027 profile numbers verified against the OFFICIAL Stern class-profile page (4,933 / 1,161 / 336; GMAT 737 10th-ed and 682 Focus with the stated ranges; GPA 3.64; 45% women; 43% international). CORRECTION 1 (high confidence): the waitlist self-improvement claim over-attributes specifics to the FAQ — the live PDF does NOT contain 'Waitlist Update Form,' does NOT mention the Executive Assessment/EA in its retake passage (only 'GMAT or GRE'), and does NOT name a 'continued-interest essay'; it directs applicants to upload via the 'application status page' and select 'Supplemental Information,' with the supplemental rec emailed to admchair@stern.nyu.edu — strip the EA/Update-Form/essay specifics or re-label them as non-FAQ inferences. CORRECTION 2 (citation): the notification-process URL should be the full-time path (/programs-admissions/full-time-mba/admissions/notification-process), not the /mba-programs/ hub. GAP (flagged focus area): the draft contains NO claim about the FT-vs-part-time test-waiver distinction, which I verified independently — FT MBA requires a test with a discretionary waiver (bachelor's required, 300-word essay, ~3-week decision), while part-time Langone has broad categorical waivers (3.20-GPA business/engineering/NYU-undergrad, military/veteran); add this. UNVERIFIED (low confidence): the specific Clear Admit LiveWire data points are self-reported, transient, and could not be re-located — treat as illustrative only, never as benchmarks. The draft's red-flag section already correctly warns about small interview-report sample size, the two GMAT scales, and re-verifying the FAQ/deadlines each cycle.

findings.dimension: Real-world NYU Stern admit/ding/waitlist data for calibration
findings.summary: The NYU Stern full-time MBA admit cloud is high and tightening. The official Class of 2027 (2025 intake, enrolled fall 2025) profile shows a record 737 average GMAT (10th edition; 80% range 690-760), a 682 average GMAT Focus (80% range 645-725), GRE averages of 164Q/163V, a 3.64 average GPA (80% range 3.36-3.89), 5.1 years average work experience, average age 28, and a 24% admit rate on a record 4,933 applications. Class of 2026 (2024 intake) was very similar: 733 GMAT, 3.64 GPA, 25% admit rate. Crucially, a large and growing share of the class did NOT submit a GMAT: for Class of 2027 only 39% submitted GMAT, 27% GRE, 6% EA, 5% MCAT/LSAT/DAT, and 23% came in on a test waiver (16% general waiver + 7% NYU-specific) — so test-waiver and alternative-test admits are a real, substantial path, not an exception. Military veterans/active-duty are 9% of the class and are explicitly courted via the Fertitta Veterans MBA Program (flat $30k/year tuition scholarship), making veteran status a meaningful tailwind. Individual self-reported data points from Clear Admit LiveWire/DecisionWire (predominantly the 2025-2026 cycle, the in-progress Class of 2028) confirm the cloud sits in the low-to-mid 700s GMAT / mid-3.6 GPA but show real variance: waitlist admits with GMAT 700/GPA 2.94 and GRE 316, interview invites on granted waivers, and dings at GMAT 615/GPA 3.74 (R4) and EA 154. An older GMAT Club decision-tracker scattergram analysis (Class of 2021, ~116 data points) quantifies conversion: 750+ ~100% interview rate, 720-749 ~71%, 700-719 ~46%, sub-700 ~16%, with interview-to-admit conversion above 85% in the 730-740 band. Net: a GMAT of ~730+ (10th ed.) or equivalent puts a candidate comfortably in the admit cloud; sub-700 is a steep climb on stats alone; veterans and strong test-waiver candidates with high GPA/quant evidence are admitted regularly. All individual data points are self-reported and unverified.

findings.claim: NYU Stern Class of 2027 (2025 intake) has a record average GMAT of 737 on the 10th edition (80% range 690-760) and 682 average on GMAT Focus (80% range 645-725); GRE averages 164Q/163V; average GPA 3.64 (80% range 3.36-3.89).
findings.evidence: Official NYU Stern full-time MBA class profile page lists these exact figures for the enrolled Class of 2027, corroborated by Clear Admit and Poets&Quants Class of 2027 writeups. This is the center of the admit/enroll cloud.

findings.claim: Class of 2027 admit rate was 24% on a record 4,933 applications (1,161 admitted, 336 enrolled); Class of 2026 was 25% on 4,550 applications (1,141 admitted, 352 enrolled).
findings.evidence: Official Stern profile and Clear Admit profiles for both classes give matching application/admit/enroll counts. Application volume is rising year over year while the admit rate holds in the mid-20s%.

findings.claim: A large share of the Class of 2027 was admitted WITHOUT a GMAT: 39% submitted GMAT, 27% GRE, 6% Executive Assessment, 5% MCAT/LSAT/DAT, and 23% came in on a test waiver (16% general waiver + 7% NYU-specific waiver).
findings.evidence: Official Stern class-profile test-submission breakdown. The Class of 2026 equivalent was 45% GMAT / 23% GRE / 8% EA / 5% other / 13% waiver-eligible / 6% waiver-secured — so the test-waiver and alternative-test population is large and growing, meaning a polished waiver/GRE/EA application is a genuinely viable admit path.

findings.claim: Individual self-reported Clear Admit LiveWire entries (2025-2026 cycle, in-progress Class of 2028) show real variance below the headline averages: a Round 1 GMAT 700 / GPA 2.94 applicant was admitted off the waitlist; a GRE 316 (R3) and GRE 319 (R3) applicant were waitlisted; granted-waiver applicants (R3, R4) received interview invites; an EA 154 (R2) applicant was rejected from the waitlist; a GMAT 615 / GPA 3.74 (R4) applicant was outright rejected.
findings.evidence: Clear Admit LiveWire NYU Stern feed entries dated May-June 2026. These are self-reported and unverified, but they map the edges of the admit cloud: low-GPA candidates can still get in with a clean GMAT and strong narrative, while sub-650 GMAT in late rounds tends to ding.

findings.claim: Clear Admit DecisionWire enrollee entries (June 2026) show admitted/enrolled students with GPAs of 3.6 and 3.58 who also held admits (some with scholarships) at Cornell Johnson, Michigan Ross, Rochester Simon ($120k-$130k), and UVA Darden — but neither reported a specific NYU Stern scholarship amount.
findings.evidence: DecisionWire enrollee entries dated June 27, 2026 (GPA 3.6) and May 27, 2026 (GPA 3.58), both NYC/NY-area, both cross-admitted to peer schools. Indicates Stern competes for candidates in the GPA 3.55-3.65 / cross-admit-with-peers band and that some enroll at Stern without (reported) merit money.

findings.claim: An older GMAT Club decision-tracker scattergram (Class of 2021, ~116 self-reported data points) shows interview-invite rate rising sharply with GMAT: 750+ ~100%, 720-749 ~71%, 700-719 ~46%, sub-700 ~16%; interview-to-admit conversion exceeded 85% in the 730-740 band.
findings.evidence: e-GMAT's analysis of the GMAT Club NYU Stern decision tracker. The cycle is several years old (Class of 2021) so absolute thresholds are dated and scores predate GMAT Focus, but the shape — steep drop-off below 700, near-certain interview at 750+ — is a useful calibration prior for the stats-only component.

findings.claim: Military veterans/active-duty are ~9% of the Class of 2027 (11% for Class of 2026) and are actively recruited via the Fertitta Veterans MBA Program, which provides a merit scholarship reducing tuition to a flat $30,000/year plus Yellow Ribbon matching and a $20k living stipend; a featured Class of 2027 admit (Tyler McLees, West Point grad, Army Infantry/Ranger officer) credits the program as the deciding factor.
findings.evidence: Official Stern Fertitta/military pages, class profiles, and a Poets&Quants 'Meet the Class of 2027' student profile. Veteran status is a documented, institutionally-supported admit tailwind at Stern, not a neutral factor.

findings.claim: Roughly 20-25% of admitted full-time two-year MBA students receive a merit-based scholarship, with Round 1/Round 2 applicants favored; named awards range from $50k-$60k/year fellowships to full-tuition Stern Scholarship and Consortium fellowships.
findings.evidence: NYU Stern scholarship pages and consulting-firm summaries. A waitlist-admit LiveWire entry explicitly declined Stern citing 'no scholarship' while holding better funding elsewhere, illustrating that scholarship outcomes drive yield and that late-round/waitlist admits often get no money.

findings.claim: Test-waiver admits are competitive and skew toward applicants who can demonstrate quant readiness another way; a GMAT waiver is considered a potential red flag for IB/consulting recruiting where a score is still expected.
findings.evidence: GMAT Club discussion threads and Stern's official waiver policy (reviewed rolling, decision within ~3 weeks). Self-reported applicant commentary indicates waiver applicants generally need a higher GPA and stronger career evidence than score-submitters. Source is community discussion, hence medium confidence.

predictorImplications: Center the admit-cloud prior on GMAT ~730-737 (10th ed.) / ~682 Focus / GRE ~164Q-163V and GPA ~3.64, with 80% GMAT range roughly 690-760 and GPA 3.36-3.89. A candidate at or above these is solidly inside the cloud; treat ~700 GMAT / ~3.4 GPA as the lower-quartile edge, not the median.

Model a steep stats-only drop-off below 700 GMAT: historical tracker data shows interview rates falling from ~71% (720-749) to ~46% (700-719) to ~16% (sub-700). For sub-700 applicants, weight non-stat factors (round, narrative, demographics, work pedigree) much more heavily.

Treat test-waiver / GRE / EA as a fully legitimate admit path — ~23% of the class came in on a waiver and only 39% submitted GMAT. Do not penalize a no-GMAT applicant categorically; instead require compensating evidence (high GPA, quant coursework/certifications, strong career accomplishments). A waiver is weakest for IB/consulting-target applicants.

Apply a meaningful positive adjustment for U.S. military veteran/active-duty status: ~9% of the class, an institutional pipeline (Fertitta program), and a near-automatic funding path ($30k flat tuition + Yellow Ribbon). Veterans with otherwise sub-median stats should be scored more favorably than non-veterans with identical stats.

Encode a strong round effect: Round 1 and Round 2 are materially better for both admit odds and scholarship; Round 3/4 admits are rarer, often waitlist-driven, and frequently come with no money. Late-round (R3/R4) sub-700 or EA-only applicants should carry a low admit probability.

Model waitlist as a live but low-yield path with wide stat variance (admits seen at GMAT 700/GPA 2.94 and GRE 316) — waitlist admits often arrive late (May) and without scholarship, which depresses yield; factor scholarship-driven declines into any enrollment model.

Account for rising selectivity year over year: applications climbed from 4,550 (C/o 2026) to 4,933 (C/o 2027) while the admit rate held ~24-25% and average GMAT hit record highs, so calibrate any 2026-2027-cycle (Class of 2028/2029) predictor toward slightly tighter thresholds than the published Class of 2027 numbers.
findings.benchmarks: Admit-cloud center (Class of 2027, enrolled fall 2025): GMAT 737 10th ed. / 682 Focus, GRE 164Q/163V, GPA 3.64, WE 5.1 yrs, age 28, 24% admit rate. 80% ranges: GMAT 10th ed. 690-760, GMAT Focus 645-725, GPA 3.36-3.89, GRE Q 160-170 / V 158-168. Test mix: 39% GMAT, 27% GRE, 6% EA, 5% MCAT/LSAT/DAT, 23% waiver (16% general + 7% NYU). Veterans 9%, women 45%, international 43%. Historical GMAT-band interview rates (GMAT Club tracker, C/o 2021, ~116 pts): 750+ ~100%, 720-749 ~71%, 700-719 ~46%, sub-700 ~16%; interview-to-admit >85% at 730-740. Prior-year baseline (C/o 2026): GMAT 733, GPA 3.64, 25% admit rate, veterans 11%.
redFlags: GMAT Club's decision tracker and individual threads, plus Reddit r/MBA, could NOT be fetched directly (403 Forbidden / blocked); GMAT Club data here comes secondhand via e-GMAT's analysis and search snippets, and the granular scattergram cited is an older Class of 2021 cycle (pre-GMAT Focus), so absolute thresholds are dated.
All individual LiveWire/DecisionWire/forum data points are SELF-REPORTED and unverified; scores, GPAs, rounds, and decisions cannot be independently confirmed and may be misremembered or embellished.

The richest set of individual data points (LiveWire/DecisionWire) is from the 2025-2026 cycle (in-progress Class of 2028), which is not yet a published class profile — these are decision-level anecdotes, not a completed cohort, and skew toward later rounds (R3/R4) and waitlist cases that happened to be reported in May-June 2026.
Sample sizes for any single GMAT/GPA cell are tiny; the cloud edges (e.g., GPA 2.94 waitlist admit, GMAT 615 ding) are individual cases, not distributions, and should not be over-fit.

No source provided a clean joint GMAT-x-GPA admitted-vs-rejected scattergram for a recent cycle; the conversion-by-band figures and the joint cloud are assembled from separate sources of differing vintage.

Scholarship amounts at NYU Stern specifically are rarely disclosed in the self-reported entries (cross-admit scholarships at other schools are reported more often), so scholarship-outcome calibration for Stern is thin and inferential.

Veteran/Fertitta admit evidence is qualitative (one named P&Q profile + program structure + 9% class share); there is no published veteran-specific stat distribution, so the magnitude of the veteran tailwind is inferred, not measured.

checks.claim: NYU Stern Class of 2027 (2025 intake, enrolled fall 2025): record average GMAT 737 (10th edition; 80% range 690-760), GMAT Focus average 682 (80% range 645-725), GRE 164Q/163V, average GPA 3.64 (80% range 3.36-3.89), 5.1 yrs work experience, average age 28.
checks.corrected: All figures match the official Stern class-profile page exactly: GMAT 737 / 80% 690-760, Focus 682 / 80% 645-725, GRE 164Q/163V, GPA 3.64 / 80% 3.36-3.89, WE 5.1 yrs, age 28. CAVEAT on the GMAT Focus RANGE: the official page and Clear Admit give the 80% range as 645-725; Poets&Quants reports a FULL range of 595-775 for the Focus edition. Both are correct but measure different things (middle-80% vs full min-max) — do not conflate them. The 737 is on the older GMAT 10th edition; the Focus average of 682 is the comparable figure for current-format test-takers.

checks.claim: Class of 2027 admit rate 24% on a record 4,933 applications (1,161 admitted, 336 enrolled); Class of 2026 was 25% on 4,550 applications (1,141 admitted, 352 enrolled).
checks.corrected: C/o 2027: 4,933 apps, 1,161 admits, 24% rate, 336 enrolled — all confirmed verbatim on the official Stern page and Clear Admit. C/o 2026: 4,550 apps, 1,141 admits, 352 enrolled, ~25% rate (1,141/4,550 = 25.1%) — confirmed by Clear Admit's C/o 2026 profile. Application volume rose ~8.4% YoY (4,550 to 4,933) and ~60% over two years (from 3,075). Note class size shrank slightly 352 to 336.

checks.claim: A large share of C/o 2027 was admitted WITHOUT a GMAT: 39% submitted GMAT, 27% GRE, 6% Executive Assessment, 5% MCAT/LSAT/DAT, and 23% on a test waiver (16% general waiver + 7% NYU-specific). C/o 2026 was 45% GMAT / 23% GRE / 8% EA / 5% other / 13% waiver-eligible / 6% waiver-secured.
checks.corrected: C/o 2027 test mix confirmed on official Stern page: GMAT 39%, GRE 27%, EA 6%, MCAT/LSAT/DAT 5%, plus two waiver lines totaling 23%. ONE LABELING NUANCE: the official page lists '16%' and '7%' as two waiver categories; Clear Admit labels them '16% test-waiver secured' and '7% test-waiver eligible' — i.e. the draft's '16% general + 7% NYU-specific' split is a reasonable but not perfectly sourced gloss of the two 16%/7% lines. The aggregate (~23% admitted on some form of waiver, only 39% submitting GMAT) is solid and is the load-bearing point. C/o 2026 mix (45/23/8/5, 13% eligible / 6% secured) confirmed by Clear Admit.

checks.claim: The FT-vs-part-time test-waiver distinction: a test waiver is genuinely available for the FULL-TIME two-year MBA (not just part-time), reviewed rolling with a decision typically within ~3 weeks.
checks.corrected: CONFIRMED and important: the official Stern standardized-tests page states a test waiver is available for the Full-Time MBA (and Luxury & Retail MBA) for the 2026-2027 cycle. Requirements: completed bachelor's, a waiver-request form, resume, transcripts, certifications, and a 300-word-max essay on coursework/certifications/experience. Decisions are rolling, 'in most cases within 3 weeks.' Waiver-request deadline is April 1, 2027. The draft's framing that a polished waiver/GRE/EA application is a viable FT admit path is well-supported — ~23% of the enrolled FT class came in on a waiver. The waiver is NOT limited to part-time; this distinction in the draft is accurate.

checks.claim: Current 2026-2027 essay prompts and word limits: Essay 1 'Professional Aspirations' (500 words, double-spaced, 12pt); Essay 2 'Personal Expression / Pick Six' (PDF, 6 images + captions + <=3-sentence intro); optional Essay 3 'Additional Information' (500 words).
checks.corrected: NOTE: the draft findings JSON does not itself assert essay prompts, but the task flagged these for verification. Confirmed from the official Stern essays page: Essay 1 'Professional Aspirations' (short-term goals + why Stern), 500 words max, double-spaced 12pt; Essay 2 'Pick Six' personal-expression PDF (intro <=3 sentences, six images, one-sentence captions); Essay 3 optional Additional Information, 500 words max. The older 'Change the World' and 'IDEA / What matters most' style prompts are NOT current — any draft language implying those would be stale. The current required prompts are Professional Aspirations + Pick Six.

checks.claim: NYU Stern Full-Time MBA 2026-2027 deadlines: R1 Sep 15, 2026; R2 Oct 15, 2026; R3 Jan 15, 2027; R4 Apr 15, 2027.
checks.corrected: Confirmed on the official Stern deadlines page (class entering Fall 2027): R1 deadline Sep 15, 2026 (notify by Dec 1, 2026); R2 Oct 15, 2026 (notify by Jan 1, 2027); R3 Jan 15, 2027 (notify by Apr 1, 2027); R4 Apr 15, 2027 (rolling notification). All due 11:59 p.m. U.S. Eastern. Caveat for calibration: only U.S. citizens/permanent residents may apply in R4 (international applicants effectively capped at R3), which sharpens the 'R4 = thin, often waitlist-driven, no money' modeling point.

checks.claim: Military veterans/active-duty are ~9% of C/o 2027 (11% C/o 2026) and are recruited via the Fertitta Veterans MBA Program: flat $30,000/year tuition scholarship plus Yellow Ribbon matching and a $20k living stipend. Featured C/o 2027 admit Tyler McLees (West Point grad, Army Infantry/Ranger officer) credits the program as the deciding factor.
checks.corrected: Veteran share 9% (C/o 2027) and 11% (C/o 2026) confirmed. Fertitta details confirmed: merit scholarship reduces tuition to $30,000/year; Yellow Ribbon up to $33,100/yr matched by VA (up to $66,200 total); $20,000/year living stipend for two-year FT MBA students using Ch.33/Yellow Ribbon who are NOT already receiving a merit scholarship (stipend and scholarship are mutually exclusive). McLees profile fully confirmed: West Point (Systems Engineering), Army Infantry commanding officer, 75th Ranger Regiment / RASP; he states 'The Fertitta Veterans MBA Program made the difference for me.' Veteran status as an institutionally-supported tailwind is well-supported.

checks.claim: Older GMAT Club scattergram (Class of 2021, ~116 data points): interview-invite rate 750+ ~100%, 720-749 ~71%, 700-719 ~46%, sub-700 ~16%; interview-to-admit conversion >85% in the 730-740 band.
checks.corrected: The SHAPE is confirmed (steep monotonic rise; 750+ ~100% / all 9 invited; sub-700 near-zero — only 1 of 25 below 680 invited; >85% interview-to-admit at GMAT 730-740) but two band figures in the draft do not match the e-GMAT source: the article reports ~60% (not 71%) for the 720-749 range and 8 of 17 = 47% (≈46%, OK) for 700-719. Sample is ~120+ data points (draft's 116 is in the right ballpark). KEY CAVEATS for use: (a) this is the Class of 2021 cycle, pre-GMAT-Focus, so absolute thresholds are dated; (b) it is e-GMAT's secondhand read of a self-reported GMAT Club tracker, not a primary admissions stat; (c) interview-invite != admit. Treat as a directional prior only, and correct the 720-749 figure from '~71%' to '~60%.'

checks.claim: Roughly 20-25% of admitted full-time two-year MBA students receive a merit-based scholarship; R1/R2 favored; named awards range from $50k-$60k/yr fellowships to full-tuition Stern Scholarship and Consortium fellowships.
checks.corrected: The official Stern scholarships pages describe merit awards (including full-tuition Stern Scholarship and Consortium fellowships) but do NOT publish a '20-25% of admits' percentage — that figure appears only in secondary/consulting summaries and search aggregations, not a primary Stern disclosure. So the existence of substantial merit aid and named fellowships is confirmed, but the specific 20-25% share is unverified against a primary source and should stay medium/low confidence. The qualitative point (R1/R2 better for scholarship; late-round/waitlist admits often get no money) is plausible and consistent with self-reported anecdotes but not independently confirmable.

checks.claim: Individual self-reported Clear Admit LiveWire/DecisionWire data points (2025-2026 cycle / in-progress Class of 2028): e.g., GMAT 700 / GPA 2.94 waitlist admit; GRE 316 and 319 waitlists; granted-waiver interview invites; EA 154 waitlist reject; GMAT 615 / GPA 3.74 R4 reject; enrollees with GPA 3.6 / 3.58 cross-admitted to peer schools.
checks.corrected: These are SELF-REPORTED, single-applicant anecdotes posted to live feeds; scores, GPAs, rounds, and outcomes cannot be independently verified and the specific dated entries cannot be reconstructed after the fact (feeds are rolling). The draft already labels them unverified, which is correct. They are usable only as illustrative cloud-edge anecdotes, NOT as data: do not treat any single cell (e.g., the GPA 2.94 waitlist admit or GMAT 615 ding) as a distribution. They also skew to late rounds (R3/R4) and waitlist cases that happened to be reported in May-June 2026, biasing toward the tail. Keep at low confidence and do not over-fit predictor thresholds to them.

verify.netAssessment: The draft's hard, checkable claims hold up very well against authoritative sources. Every official class-profile number for the Class of 2027 (enrolled fall 2025) was confirmed verbatim on the NYU Stern class-profile page and corroborated by Clear Admit and Poets&Quants: GMAT 737 (10th ed., 80% range 690-760), GMAT Focus 682 (80% range 645-725), GRE 164Q/163V, GPA 3.64 (80% range 3.36-3.89), WE 5.1 yrs, age 28, 24% admit rate on 4,933 apps (1,161 admitted, 336 enrolled), 9% veterans. The Class of 2026 comparison (733 GMAT, 3.64 GPA, ~25% rate on 4,550 apps, 11% veterans) and both test-submission breakdowns are also confirmed. FOUR things to flag for the writer. (1) The FT-vs-part-time test-waiver distinction is CORRECT and well-supported: the official standardized-tests page confirms a waiver is genuinely available for the Full-Time MBA, decided rolling within ~3 weeks, deadline April 1, 2027 — ~23% of the enrolled FT class came in on a waiver, so it is a real admit path, not part-time-only. (2) The GMAT Focus RANGE has a measurement ambiguity: 645-725 is the middle-80% (official/Clear Admit), while Poets&Quants' 595-775 is the full min-max — both are right but must not be conflated. (3) The e-GMAT scattergram needs a numeric correction: the 720-749 interview rate is ~60% in the source, not the draft's ~71% (700-719 ~46-47% and the 750+/sub-700/85%-conversion-at-730-740 points check out); and it is a dated, pre-Focus, secondhand, self-reported Class-of-2021 read — directional prior only. (4) The current essay prompts are Professional Aspirations (500w) + Pick Six + optional Additional Info (500w); any 'Change the World'-era prompt language would be stale. Weaker items, correctly flagged: the '20-25% receive merit aid' figure is not on a primary Stern page (medium); all LiveWire/DecisionWire individual data points are self-reported and effectively unverifiable (low). Net: center the admit cloud on the confirmed C/o 2027 numbers, label cycles carefully (737 is 10th-edition; 682 Focus is the current-format comparator), fix the 720-749 figure, and keep the anecdote- and scholarship-percentage claims at the low/medium confidence they deserve.

findings.dimension: How NYU Stern evaluates U.S. military/veteran applicants, and the Fertitta Veterans Program
findings.summary: NYU Stern is one of the most veteran-committed M7-adjacent programs, anchored by the Fertitta Veterans MBA Program — established in 2016 via a $15M endowment from brothers Lorenzo (MBA '93) and Frank J. Fertitta III, and described by Stern as the only program of its kind at a U.S. business school. The program is open to veterans and active-duty service members admitted to the Two-Year Full-Time MBA, and combines three layers of money plus a veteran-designed six-week summer session and a student-run Military Veterans Club (MVC). Critically, admission to Stern is the gate — there is no separate Fertitta application; admitted veterans are then considered for the merit-based Fertitta Scholarship (reduces tuition to a flat $30,000/year), but not all admitted veterans receive it. Military veterans/active-duty were 11% of the Class of 2026 and 9% of the Class of 2027, so this is a sizable, competitive internal sub-pool. Consultants and Service to School are consistent that military background alone is not an edge: veteran test scores track class averages (Stern's Class of 2027 GMAT averaged a record 737), MOS/branch does not differentiate, and the deciding factors are academics plus a translated, civilian-legible, quantified-impact narrative with a specific (non-default-consulting) goal. The dominant failure modes are jargon/acronym overload, leading with "readiness" instead of quantified business impact, defaulting to consulting (~80% of veteran clients start there, ~30% end up there), exclusive focus on the military with no broader/community dimension, and sounding like every other transitioning officer. Standing out vs. the large logistics/operations-officer sub-pool comes from quantified P&L/cost/efficiency results, a differentiated and credible post-MBA path (supply chain, defense tech, corporate strategy, entrepreneurship, finance), and strong academics — not from the role itself.

findings.claim: The Fertitta Veterans MBA Program was established at NYU Stern in 2016 via a $15 million endowment from alumnus Lorenzo Fertitta (MBA '93) and his brother Frank J. Fertitta III; Stern describes it as believed to be the only program of its kind at a U.S. business school, exclusively for military veteran and active-duty students entering the Full-Time MBA.
findings.evidence: Stern's official Fertitta page and Stern Business magazine both state the 2016 founding and $15M endowment from Lorenzo (MBA '93) and Frank J. Fertitta III, and frame it as the only program of its kind at a US business school for veteran/active-duty FT MBA students.

findings.claim: The Fertitta Veterans Scholarship is a merit-based award that reduces tuition to a flat $30,000 per academic year. All admitted active-duty/veteran students are eligible to be considered (regardless of summer-program participation), but not all admitted veterans receive it, and there is no separate scholarship application — applicants indicate interest in merit scholarships within the standard application.
findings.evidence: Stern Fertitta page: scholarship 'reduces tuition to $30,000 per academic year'; 'not all military veterans and active-duty students who are admitted into Stern will be awarded the Fertitta Scholarship'; no separate application — applicants check the merit-scholarship interest box.

findings.claim: Beyond the Fertitta Scholarship, Stern layers Yellow Ribbon (Stern contributes up to $33,100/academic year, VA-matched for up to $66,200/year per eligible student under Chapter 33/Post-9/11) and a $20,000/year taxable living stipend for Two-Year FT MBA students using Chapter 33/Yellow Ribbon/VA benefits who are NOT already receiving a merit scholarship. Application fee waivers are available to active-duty/veteran applicants with service documentation.
findings.evidence: Stern pages list Yellow Ribbon up to $33,100/year (total up to $66,200 with VA match), a $20,000/year taxable living stipend for VA-benefit users not receiving merit aid, and fee waivers for veterans/active-duty with documentation.

findings.claim: The Fertitta summer program runs ~six weeks (early July to mid-August) and includes two core courses for 6 credits — Statistics & Data Analysis and Accounting (Financial Accounting) — plus career programming, corporate visits (consulting, banking, technology firms), veteran alumni mentorship, and social events, culminating in LAUNCH (Stern's week-long MBA orientation). There is no separate application; admitted veterans indicate preference after the admission interview, and scholarship/stipend eligibility is independent of whether a student joins the summer session.
findings.evidence: Stern Fertitta page: six weeks, two core courses (Statistics & Data Analysis, Accounting, 6 credits), career development, corporate visits, alumni networking; preference indicated after admission interview, no separate application. Stern Business magazine describes accounting + statistics courses, visits to top consulting/banking/tech firms, alumni mentors, and the LAUNCH orientation.

findings.claim: The inaugural (2016) Fertitta summer cohort included 25 students (a small number of whom were non-scholars). This gives a rough sense of cohort scale, though Stern does not publish a fixed annual number of Fertitta scholars.
findings.evidence: Stern Business magazine and search results indicate the first summer session welcomed 25 students, including a few non-scholars. No fixed recurring scholar count is published, so current-cycle cohort size is not confirmable.

findings.claim: U.S. military veterans/active-duty made up 11% of the NYU Stern Full-Time MBA Class of 2026 (class size 352; 4,550 applications; ~31% acceptance; 47% women; 40% international; avg GPA 3.64; avg GMAT 733; ~5 years work experience).
findings.evidence: Clear Admit Class of 2026 profile: 'U.S. military veterans and active-duty members lands at 11 percent of the class'; 352 enrolled; 4,550 apps; 47% women; 40% international; GPA 3.64; GMAT 733.

findings.claim: U.S. military veterans/active-duty made up 9% of the NYU Stern Full-Time MBA Class of 2027 — a decline from 11% in the Class of 2026 (class size 336; 4,933 applications; 24% acceptance; 45% women; 43% international; avg GPA 3.64; avg GMAT 737, a school record; avg 5.1 years work experience).
findings.evidence: Clear Admit Class of 2027 profile: 'Nine percent of the class' is military veterans/active-duty; 336 enrolled; 4,933 apps; 24% acceptance; 45% women; 43% international citizenship across 43 countries; GPA 3.64; GMAT 737 ('a record high for the school'); 5.1 years experience.

findings.claim: The Military Veterans Club (MVC) is a student-run organization providing networking, mentorship, and professional exposure — career panels for first-year MBAs, a speaker series with veteran business leaders, charity fundraising (e.g., at the NY Mercantile Exchange) for injured veterans, and a skills workshop for foreign military service members. The MVC and its Fertitta resources serve as a support/networking hub, not an admissions decision-maker.
findings.evidence: Stern military community page describes MVC career panels, veteran-leader speaker series, NYMEX charity fundraising, and an international-officer skills workshop. The MVC's Fertitta page functions as an informational/resource hub linking to financial-aid and GI Bill resources.

findings.claim: AdCom and consultants agree military leadership/operational responsibility translates well to what MBA programs want, but veterans must explicitly translate stories into civilian terms — AdComs will NOT automatically grasp the depth of military experience. Stern partners with Service to School (S2S), the free veteran application-counseling nonprofit, and veterans are urged to have non-military readers and target-school veterans' clubs review essays for jargon.
findings.evidence: Service to School: officer responsibilities 'often translate well' but 'don't assume Adcoms will automatically understand the depth of your experience. Translate your stories into terms they'll understand.' Stern partners with S2S. mbaMission/Stacy Blackman advise removing acronyms/jargon and having civilian/veteran-club readers proofread.

findings.claim: Military background alone is not an admissions edge: veteran applicants' test scores closely track overall class averages at leading programs, and it is academics/test scores — not the MOS or branch — that differentiate admitted veterans. Strong GMAT/GRE plus a clear, specific career vision drive outcomes more than the specialty itself.
findings.evidence: Service to School: 'veteran applicants' test scores closely align with the overall class averages'; 'it's not the MOS itself that gives these candidates an edge—it's their academic performance and test scores.'

findings.claim: The most common veteran-essay failure modes: (1) copying a peer's 'buddy' strategy instead of authentic self-reflection; (2) leading with operational 'readiness' instead of quantified business impact (rewrite 'Maintained 95% operational readiness' as 'reduced downtime by 40% ... saving $200K annually'); (3) treating veterans' clubs as gatekeepers (they make no admissions decisions); (4) defaulting to consulting as the goal (~80% of military clients start there, only ~30% end up there); (5) underestimating interview prep. Veterans also commonly over-focus on military service and omit broader community/extracurricular engagement.
findings.evidence: Poets&Quants (Emily Sawyer Kegerreis): five mistakes — buddy-strategy copying; leading with 'readiness' vs. quantified impact (40% downtime / $200K example); clubs are 'NOT gatekeepers'; ~80% start wanting consulting but only ~30% do it; underestimating interview prep ('100% of our HBS and Stanford interview prep clients were admitted'). Service to School flags exclusive military focus / lack of community involvement.

findings.claim: To stand out vs. the large logistics/operations-officer sub-pool rather than blend in, veterans should quantify P&L/cost/efficiency results (dollars saved, downtime reduced, mission/throughput improved), articulate a specific and credible non-default post-MBA path (supply chain, defense technology, corporate strategy, entrepreneurship, or finance) connected to their experience, demonstrate dimensions beyond the job (community, mentorship), and back it with strong academics — since admitted veterans' metrics track class averages.
findings.evidence: Poets&Quants advises quantified impact over readiness language and exploring supply chain, defense tech, corporate strategy, and entrepreneurship rather than default consulting. Service to School emphasizes academics + differentiated narrative + community over MOS, since scores track class averages.

predictorImplications: Veteran status is a recognized strength at Stern but NOT a standalone differentiator: because Stern's Class of 2027 GMAT averaged a record 737 and veteran scores track class averages, a predictor model should weight GMAT/GRE and GPA heavily for veteran applicants rather than treating military service as a thumb on the scale.

Model the Fertitta Scholarship as conditional, not automatic: admission is the gate (no separate Fertitta application), and Stern explicitly states not all admitted veterans receive the scholarship — so 'admitted veteran' and 'Fertitta scholar' are distinct outcomes that should be predicted separately.

Penalize generic/default goals: a stated post-MBA goal of 'consulting' with no specific, experience-linked rationale is a documented blend-in signal (~80% start there, ~30% end up there). Reward specific, credible paths (supply chain, defense tech, corporate strategy, finance, entrepreneurship).

Reward quantified-impact narratives over operational/'readiness' language and over jargon density — civilian-legible, dollar/percent-anchored accomplishment statements are the consultant-cited differentiator. Acronym/jargon-heavy material is a negative signal.

Treat the logistics/operations-officer profile as a crowded sub-pool: among such applicants, differentiation should be modeled via quantified financial/efficiency outcomes, broader community/extracurricular signal, and a non-default goal — not via MOS or branch.

Class-mix is shifting: veteran share fell from 11% (Class of 2026) to 9% (Class of 2027), so any base-rate assumptions about veteran representation should use the most recent (Class of 2027) figure and flag the downward trend.

Veterans' clubs (MVC) and Service to School are support/coaching signals, not gatekeepers — do not treat club engagement as an admissions-decision input, but do treat coached, civilian-reviewed essays as a quality proxy.
redFlags: Stern does not publish a fixed annual number of Fertitta scholars; the only concrete cohort figure found is the 2016 inaugural summer session of 25 students (including a few non-scholars), which is dated and may not reflect current cohort size — treat current scholar counts as not confirmable (low confidence).

The 'reduces tuition to $30,000/year' figure and the $33,100 Yellow Ribbon / $20,000 stipend amounts are current on Stern's pages as of this 2026 research but are subject to annual change; verify against the live Stern Fertitta/financial-aid pages for the specific application cycle.

The ~80%-start-consulting / ~30%-end-consulting and '100% of HBS/Stanford interview-prep clients admitted' figures come from a single named consultant (Emily Sawyer Kegerreis via Poets&Quants) and reflect that firm's client base — they are illustrative, not Stern-specific or independently audited.

Service to School's claim that 'MOS doesn't matter' and that veteran scores track class averages is general MBA-landscape guidance, not NYU Stern-specific data; no Stern-published breakdown of veteran academic stats vs. the broader class was found.

No source provides a NYU Stern-specific statement of how AdCom weights military experience or what distinguishes a Fertitta scholar from a non-scholar admit; the evaluation guidance is synthesized from cross-school consultant/nonprofit sources and should be labeled as general, not Stern-official.

Class profile percentages are reported by Clear Admit; while reliable and consistent, they are secondary reporting of Stern data rather than a direct Stern class-profile page citation for the veteran percentage specifically.
findings.benchmarks: Veteran share of Full-Time MBA: 11% (Class of 2026), 9% (Class of 2027). Class of 2027 overall benchmarks (the relevant comparison bar for a veteran applicant): class size 336; 4,933 applications; 24% acceptance; avg GMAT 737 (10th edition, school record); avg GPA 3.64; 45% women; 43% international; 5.1 years avg work experience. Class of 2026: size 352; 4,550 apps; ~31% acceptance; avg GMAT 733; avg GPA 3.64; 47% women; 40% international; ~5 years experience. Fertitta financial structure: tuition reduced to a flat $30,000/year (merit Fertitta Scholarship, not guaranteed to all admitted veterans); Yellow Ribbon up to $33,100/year from Stern matched by VA for up to $66,200/year total; $20,000/year taxable living stipend for VA-benefit users not on merit aid. Summer program: ~6 weeks (early July–mid-August), 2 core courses / 6 credits (Statistics & Data Analysis, Accounting), culminating in LAUNCH orientation. Consultant-cited behavioral benchmark: ~80% of veteran clients initially target consulting vs. ~30% who ultimately pursue it.

checks.claim: Fertitta Veterans MBA Program established 2016 via $15M endowment from Lorenzo Fertitta (MBA '93) and brother Frank J. Fertitta III; Stern describes it as believed to be the only program of its kind at a US business school for veteran/active-duty FT MBA students.

The exact 'Believed to be the only program of its kind at a U.S. business school, according to Stern' phrasing is confirmed verbatim in Clear Admit's 2016 piece quoting Stern. checks.claim: Fertitta Scholarship reduces tuition to a flat $30,000/academic year; not all admitted veterans receive it; no separate scholarship application (applicants check the merit-scholarship interest box).
checks.corrected: Confirmed verbatim from Stern's live Fertitta page: tuition reduced to '$30,000 per academic year'; 'not all military veterans and active-duty students who are admitted into Stern will be awarded the Fertitta Scholarship'; no separate application, applicants 'check the box indicating interest in being considered for merit-based scholarships.'

checks.claim: Stern Yellow Ribbon contribution up to $33,100/academic year, VA-matched for total up to $66,200/year per eligible Chapter 33 student; plus a $20,000/year taxable living stipend for Two-Year FT MBA students using VA benefits who are NOT receiving a merit scholarship; application fee waivers available to active-duty/veteran applicants with documentation.
checks.corrected: All figures confirmed verbatim on Stern pages: Yellow Ribbon 'up to $33,100 per academic year' matched by VA for 'a total award of up to $66,200 per year per student'; '$20,000 per academic year, subject to tax' stipend for Two-Year FT students on Chapter 33/Yellow Ribbon benefits without merit aid; fee waiver for 'U.S. military members and U.S. military veterans who have served on active duty' with documentation emailed to admchair@stern.nyu.edu. However, the 'including a few non-scholars' detail is NOT supported by the Stern Business magazine page, which does not break out scholars vs. non-scholars (one search snippet asserted 'three non-scholars,' but this could not be confirmed on a primary Stern source — treat as unverified). Separately, the 2016 launch projection was 'around 20 incoming students eligible for the Fertitta Veterans Program each year' (Poets&Quants/Clear Admit) — a useful scale figure the draft omits. checks.claim: Military veterans/active-duty were 11% of the Full-Time MBA Class of 2026; class size 352; 4,550 applications; ~31% acceptance; 47% women; 40% international; avg GPA 3.64; avg GMAT 733; ~5 years work experience.

checks.corrected: All five mistakes and quotes confirmed verbatim, including 'reduced downtime by 40%...saving $200K annually,' 'Veterans Clubs are NOT gatekeepers,' ~80% start/30% finish consulting, and '100% of our HBS and Stanford interview prep clients were admitted.' Author is Emily Sawyer Kegerreis, Founder of Military MBA Consulting. Correctly flagged as single-firm, client-base data — illustrative, not Stern-specific or audited.
