Confidence medium because this is synthesized cross-school guidance, not a Stern-official statement of how AdCom weights military experience — correctly labeled as general in the draft's own red flags.

checks.claim: [NOT IN DRAFT — GAP] The military/veteran standardized-test waiver and the FT-vs-part-time distinction.
checks.corrected: Important veteran-lens gap the draft never addresses. Stern's U.S. military/veteran STANDARDIZED-TEST WAIVER applies ONLY to the part-time Langone MBA: 'Active members of the U.S. military and U.S. veterans can waive the standardized test requirement for the Langone Part-time MBA program' (only requirement: proof of service). The Full-Time (Two-Year) MBA has NO military-specific test waiver — its waivers are the general academic-readiness waiver and the NYU-undergrad-GPA-3.20 waiver. Since the Fertitta Program and all class-profile/GMAT discussion concern the FULL-TIME MBA, a veteran FT applicant must still submit a test or qualify for a general (non-military) waiver. Any model or guidance must not assume a military test waiver for the full-time program.

verify.netAssessment: The draft is highly accurate and well-sourced. Of 12 substantive claims, 9 are confirmed verbatim against primary Stern pages and the cited secondary sources, and the heavily caveated single-firm consultant figures (Poets&Quants) and general S2S guidance check out exactly as quoted. Two corrections and one gap matter for the veteran lens: (1) The Class of 2026 acceptance rate is wrong — Stern admitted 1,141 of 4,550 = ~25%, not the draft's '~31%' (the Class of 2027's 24%, by contrast, is correct). (2) The '25 inaugural students including a few non-scholars' detail overstates what primary sources support: the 25 figure is solid, but the scholar/non-scholar split is not confirmable on a Stern source (mark unverified); the draft also omits the launch-era projection of '~20 scholars per year,' the only published scale figure. (3) Most consequential gap: the task-flagged FT-vs-part-time test-waiver distinction is entirely absent. Stern's military/veteran test waiver is a PART-TIME (Langone) policy only; the Full-Time MBA — which is what the Fertitta Program, class profiles, and the record 737 GMAT all refer to — has no military-specific test waiver. A veteran applying full-time must still test or use a general waiver, so neither the narrative nor any predictor model should assume a military test waiver applies to the full-time pool. All class stats are correctly labeled by cycle (Class of 2026 vs 2027), and the GMAT 737 record (Class of 2027) and 11%->9% veteran-share decline are accurate.

findings.dimension: How expert admissions consultants convert an applicant profile into an admit-probability estimate (methodology for calibrating a complete file to a probability for a school like NYU Stern)
findings.summary: Across ARINGO, Menlo Coaching, Stacy Blackman (SBC), and mbaMission, there is a shared, reconstructable methodology even though only ARINGO publishes an actual numeric model. The logic has six layers. (1) ANCHOR ON THE POOL, NOT THE PERSON: start from the school's structural admit rate as a base rate, then adjust. For NYU Stern, the relevant base is ~24% overall admit rate (Class of 2027: 4,933 apps / 1,161 admits / 336 enrolled, 28.9% yield) — but every firm warns the headline rate is NOT an individual's odds. (2) ACADEMICS AS A THRESHOLD/GATE, NOT A LINEAR SCORER: GMAT/GRE/GPA function as a viability filter against the 80% range, not a continuous probability driver. Once a candidate clears the analytical-readiness bar (and offsets exist below it), additional points add little. Stern Class of 2027 calibration points: GMAT (10th ed.) avg 737 / range 660-770; GMAT Focus avg 682 / 80% range ~595-775; GPA avg 3.64 / 80% range ~3.36-3.89; GRE avg ~327. (3) SEGMENT THE POOL: the single biggest swing on individual odds is over- vs under-representation. Consultants/bankers/engineers compete inside a saturated micro-pool where effective odds run well below headline; differentiated profiles run above. (4) STORY/DIFFERENTIATION/FIT MOVE THE NUMBER MOST once stats clear the gate: clear realistic goals, leadership evidence, authentic self-aware essays, credible recommenders, and demonstrated fit. SBC's top rejection reasons are 3-of-4 story/positioning, not stats. (5) ROUND adjusts at the margin, profile-dependent: R1≈R2 for most; R1 materially helps overrepresented applicants; R3 is a long shot except for rare/specific profiles. (6) OUTPUT AS A CALIBRATED BAND, NOT FALSE PRECISION: ARINGO maps to ~10% (stretch) / ~50% (competitive) / ~75-80% (strong + scholarship); Menlo uses 5 verbal tiers (highly unlikely → reach → on-target → safe → practically guaranteed); SBC/mbaMission use dream/reach-target-safety with a "profile in middle-80% = target, in top-10% = safety" rule. Menlo explicitly attacks single-number outputs like "37% at Wharton" as false precision. The defensible synthesis for a predictor: base rate × stats-gate (pass/penalty) × pool-segment multiplier × story/fit multiplier × round adjustment, expressed as a band.

findings.claim: ARINGO's calculator uses ~40+ categorical inputs (GPA bands 2.6-Summa, GMAT/GRE/GMAT Focus, undergrad prestige tier, total work experience, direct reports, brand-name/military employer, promotions, essay/recommendation/resume quality each rated strong-medium-weak, round, awards, community service, athletics, entrepreneurship, age, gender, geography) and outputs a numerical score compared to school-specific point thresholds — not a probability per se, but it explicitly translates to bands: 'stretch' ~10%, 'competitive' ~50%, 'strong with scholarship potential' ~75-80%.
findings.evidence: ARINGO's calculator page enumerates these inputs and rates essays/recs/resume as strong/medium/weak and round (first/second/final). The firm characterizes results as 'stretch' (~10%), 'competitive' (~50%), and 'strong with scholarship potential' (~75-80%), while stating it is 'a rough estimate only' that 'does not guarantee actual outcomes.' It applies school-specific adjustments (e.g., a Harvard base-point subtraction) and notes GMAT band changes around 690-700 'can have a meaningful impact.'

findings.claim: ARINGO's published 'admission rates' are CLIENT-OUTCOME rates (its consulting clients' success), not raw school admit rates, and must not be confused with a school's selectivity. ~84% of qualifying ARINGO clients (7+ hours of consulting) were admitted to at least one program in the 2025 cycle; per-school client admit figures (e.g., Harvard 43%, Stanford 47%, Booth 55%, Kellogg 68%, Columbia 54%, MIT Sloan 48%) are self-reported client results.
findings.evidence: ARINGO's statistics page frames the numbers as outcomes for '100% of qualifying clients' rather than a school's applicant pool, and reports 84% admitted to at least one MBA program. It does NOT segment admit rates by GMAT band or GPA range, so it provides no public base-rate-by-segment table.

findings.claim: Menlo Coaching explicitly rejects single-number probability outputs (e.g., '37% chance at Wharton') as scientifically misleading 'false precision,' and instead places candidates into five verbal tiers: highly unlikely, reach, on-target, safe, practically guaranteed. Their 'human-powered' method gathers detailed context because admissions is holistic; they cite cases of applicants with felonies/unemployment/failed degrees admitted to M7 and 'great profiles' dinged for bad goals/attitude/storytelling.
findings.evidence: Menlo's 'Human-Powered MBA Admissions Calculator' page criticizes automated tools that merely check whether an applicant beats school averages on GMAT/GPA, attended a prestige undergrad, worked at a brand employer, applied R1, and adjust for gender — and offers categorical placement instead of percentages.

findings.claim: The dominant individual-odds modifier across firms is pool segmentation (over- vs under-representation), which can move effective odds far from the headline rate. Menlo: overrepresented applicants 'especially' must apply R1 because schools fill those cohorts early; a strong under-represented applicant may still have good odds even in R3. SBC lists 'overrepresented demographic' (consultant/banker/engineer) as a top rejection reason.
findings.evidence: Menlo's round guide states overrepresented applicants face higher within-cohort competition by later rounds, while under-represented strong applicants remain sought in R3. SBC's rejection-reasons article names overrepresentation among consultants/bankers/engineers as the #1 listed reason.

findings.claim: Stats (GMAT/GRE/GPA) are treated as a viability THRESHOLD measured against the school's 80% range, not a continuous probability driver. SBC: once the analytical-readiness bar is met the score becomes far less important ('what you have done relative to what you have been given' matters more); a sub-median score is workable, but being ~50 points below the lower bound of the 80% range signals dropping to lower-ranked options. SBC has placed sub-3.0 GPA clients at target programs.
findings.evidence: SBC's low-GMAT article frames scores against the 80% range (e.g., historic Wharton 700-770, Columbia 680-760), advises retake decisions near the lower bound and reconsidering school tier if ~50 points below it, and quotes a former AdCom that once the threshold is met 'the test score becomes meaningless.' SBC's GPA guidance cites accepting sub-3.0 clients by offsetting with GMAT, recs, essays.

findings.claim: Sub-median or low-stat profiles are calibrated by looking for OFFSETS, not by mechanically lowering the probability: quantitative job track record, strong GPA from a respected school (or vice versa), a college calculus/quant course with B+ or better, recommenders who vouch for quant aptitude with examples, and essays/extracurriculars in the applicant's control. SBC frames the score gap as 'context-able' — a low stat WITHOUT context is a rejection driver, but a low stat WITH context can be overcome.
findings.evidence: SBC advises offsetting low scores with quant work history, high GPA, leadership, supplemental calculus (B+ or better), and recommender support; it lists 'weak stats without context' as a distinct rejection reason, implying context neutralizes much of the penalty.

findings.claim: No-test / test-waiver profiles are treated as a conditional penalty, riskiest for overrepresented or quant-light applicants. Consensus across mbaMission/Menlo-adjacent sources: most admits to waiver-offering schools still submitted a score; waiving is riskier if you are from an overrepresented industry/demographic (you compete against similar applicants who DO submit). A waiver typically requires a separate academic-readiness essay and may be denied if quant background is thin.
findings.evidence: Search synthesis of mbaMission/test-optional guidance: 'most admits to schools that offered test waivers still submitted a score'; waiving is 'riskier if you are applying from an overrepresented industry or demographic'; waiver applicants often must submit a separate essay demonstrating academic readiness. Menlo's waiver page advises against waiving at competitive programs because a strong score is 'a powerful differentiator' that can 'offset other weaknesses,' and notes M7/peers generally require scores.

findings.claim: Story / differentiation / fit are the largest movers of the probability once stats clear the gate. SBC's four AdCom evaluation areas are (1) work experience + realistic goals, (2) leadership (formal title not required), (3) creativity/intellectual aptitude, (4) interpersonal skills/fit ('will others want to work with this person'); and 3 of its 4 top rejection reasons are story/positioning (overrepresentation, lack of self-awareness, generic essays) rather than raw stats. mbaMission calls a great essay 'the most significant way' to increase odds.
findings.evidence: SBC's candidacy-evaluation article lists the four focus areas and stresses authenticity and trajectory over perfection, with fit weighted 'as much as concrete qualifications.' Its rejection-reasons article: even applicants with stellar scores/leadership/recs get dinged for poor positioning or saturation. mbaMission's odds-boosting guidance centers the essay.

findings.claim: Reach/Target/Safety is defined statistically AND holistically. SBC: TARGET = your profile falls within the middle-80% range of admits and your GMAT/GRE+GPA are competitive with published ranges; REACH/DREAM = stats at/below median (one stat low, the other strong) but you have differentiators, or the school has single/low-double-digit acceptance rates; SAFETY = your profile exceeds the typical admit (but can still ding you). mbaMission recommends a 5-7 school list (a couple stretch, a couple on-target, a couple confident). Menlo notes 'target' = stats within ~10 GMAT points and ~0.2 GPA of the average.
findings.evidence: SBC's school-selection article defines target as middle-80% fit, reach as at/below median with differentiators, safety as exceeding the typical admit. mbaMission advises 5-7 schools split across stretch/on-target/confident. Menlo/USNews framing: target = GMAT within ~10 points and GPA within ~0.2 of average.

findings.claim: Round timing is a marginal, profile-dependent adjustment — not a large base-rate shift. SBC: R1 and R2 odds roughly balance (R1 has fewer but stronger applicants; apply when your app is strongest); R3 has far fewer seats and suits only very specific/rare backgrounds; reapplicants should target no later than R1 and show genuine improvement. Menlo: there is 'no single R1 vs R2 acceptance rate'; R1 helps overrepresented applicants most because no cohort seats are filled yet, and schools have more flexibility on lower GMATs in R1.
findings.evidence: SBC states R1≈R2 for most, R3 is for narrow profiles, and reapplicants should apply by R1 with demonstrated growth (citing a R2 reapplicant admitted to HBS/GSB/Wharton). Menlo states round impact is profile-dependent, R1 removes within-cohort fill risk, and schools have 'more flexibility to admit students with lower GMAT scores' in R1.

findings.claim: NYU Stern Class of 2027 (2024-25 cycle, entered fall 2025) calibration anchors: 4,933 applications, 1,161 admits, ~24% admit rate, 336 enrolled, 28.9% yield; GMAT 10th-edition average 737 (school record, up from 733 for Class of 2026), 10th-ed range 660-770; GMAT Focus average 682, Focus 80% range ~595-775; average GPA 3.64 (80% range ~3.18-4.00, mid-80% ~3.36-3.89); GRE average ~327 (V163/Q164), 27% submitted GRE; 45% women; 43% international.
findings.evidence: Poets&Quants and Clear Admit Class of 2027 profiles report 4,933 apps / 1,161 admits / 336 enrolled, 24% admit rate, 28.9% yield, GMAT 10th-ed avg 737 (range 660-770), Focus avg 682 (range 595-775), GRE avg 327, GPA avg 3.64, 45% women, 43% international. Class of 2026 comparison: 4,550 apps / 1,141 admits / 352 enrolled, GPA 3.64, 47% women, 40% international, GMAT 733.

findings.claim: Menlo lists NYU Stern's historical overall acceptance rates (25% in 2024, 31% in 2023, 27% in 2022, 19% in 2021, 29% in 2020) and warns the headline rate is 'not an indicator, one way or another, of your fit' — i.e., it is a base rate to be adjusted, not an individual probability. No firm publishes a per-segment (GMAT-band or industry) admit-rate table, so segment multipliers must be inferred, not read off a published number.
findings.evidence: Menlo's acceptance-rates page lists Stern 25%/31%/27%/19%/29% (2024-2020) and explicitly cautions that acceptance rates do not represent an individual's odds or fit. No source in this research provides admit rates broken out by GMAT band or applicant segment.

predictorImplications: Model the estimate as a multiplicative adjustment off a base rate, not a lookup: P(admit) ≈ base_rate(school, round) × stats_gate × pool_segment_multiplier × story_fit_multiplier × execution. For Stern use ~24% (Class of 2027) as the school base rate, then adjust — never report the base rate as the candidate's odds.

Treat GMAT/GRE/GPA as a GATE with a penalty curve, not a linear scorer. If the candidate is inside Stern's 80% ranges (GMAT Focus ~595-775 / 10th-ed 660-770; GPA ~3.36-3.89), apply ~no stats penalty. If below the lower bound, apply a graduated penalty that scales with distance and is dampened by offsets; SBC's heuristic: ~50 points below the 80%-range floor signals the school should drop a tier.

Make pool segmentation the largest single modifier. Flag overrepresented buckets (consulting, banking, software engineering, Indian/Chinese male engineers) and apply a downward multiplier; apply an upward multiplier for differentiated/under-represented profiles. This is the factor most likely to move Stern odds far from 24%.

Encode an offsets engine for sub-median stats: quant-heavy job, strong GPA (or vice versa), supplemental calculus/quant course with B+ or better, recommender testimony on quant ability, and quantitative essay framing. Presence of offsets should shrink the stats penalty; a low stat WITH no context should be penalized hardest (it is an explicit rejection driver).

Treat a no-test/waiver profile as a conditional penalty: small or neutral for under-represented/quant-strong applicants who qualify for a waiver, larger for overrepresented or quant-light applicants (who compete against peers submitting scores). Require an academic-readiness signal before reducing the penalty.

Weight story/differentiation/fit as the top probability mover once the stats gate is cleared, operationalized via four scorable sub-factors (realistic+specific goals, leadership evidence, self-aware/authentic essays, credible recommenders + demonstrated school fit). Three of SBC's four top ding reasons live here, so a weak story should be able to pull an otherwise on-target profile down to 'reach.'

Apply round as a small, profile-conditional adjustment: ~neutral R1-vs-R2 for most; a modest R1 boost for overrepresented candidates; a steep R3 penalty except for rare/specific profiles; a reapplicant flag requiring demonstrated improvement.

Output a calibrated BAND with a verbal tier, never a single false-precision percentage. Recommended bands mirroring the firms: stretch/reach ~10-20%, competitive/on-target ~40-60%, strong/safe ~75-80%+. Menlo explicitly condemns outputs like '37%,' so present a range plus an explicit confidence note.

Define category cutoffs the way the firms do: TARGET = profile inside the middle-80% of admits (Menlo: GMAT within ~10 points and GPA within ~0.2 of the average); SAFETY = profile in roughly the top decile of admits; REACH = at/below median with differentiators or at a sub-15% acceptance-rate school. Stern at ~24% is a TARGET for an on-profile candidate, a REACH for a sub-median/overrepresented one.

Do NOT use ARINGO's published client admit rates (e.g., '84% admitted to at least one program') as school base rates — they are self-selected consulting-client outcomes. Use independently reported school admit rates (P&Q/Clear Admit/school class profile) for base rates and ARINGO only for the band-mapping logic (10%/50%/75-80%).
redFlags: No firm publishes admit rates segmented by GMAT band, GPA range, industry, or demographic — the segment multipliers central to this methodology must be INFERRED from qualitative guidance, so any per-segment number a predictor uses is an estimate, not a sourced figure (mark confidence accordingly).

ARINGO's calculator is the only one exposing an actual numeric model, but its exact weights, the '99-line' regression, and the school-specific point thresholds (e.g., the Harvard base-point subtraction) are not publicly documented in verifiable detail; the 10%/50%/75-80% band mapping is the firm's own rough characterization and is explicitly labeled 'not a guarantee.'
ARINGO's headline success statistics are client-outcome rates, not school selectivity; conflating them with base rates would massively overstate odds. This is an easy and consequential error to make.

Menlo, SBC, and mbaMission deliberately avoid numeric probabilities and warn that single-number outputs are false precision — so a predictor that emits a point estimate is going against the explicit stated methodology of three of the four firms; bands + confidence are the defensible form.

Stern's GMAT figures are reported in two incompatible scales (10th-edition avg 737 vs GMAT Focus avg 682) plus GRE — a predictor must normalize to one scale or it will badly miscalibrate the stats gate.

Round-effect magnitude is asserted qualitatively but never quantified by any firm ('no single R1 vs R2 rate'); any numeric round multiplier is an inference. R3 'long shot' and overrepresented-R1-advantage are directional, not measured.

Cycle labeling matters: Stern Class of 2027 (entered fall 2025, 2024-25 cycle) vs Class of 2026 (GMAT 733) — these are close but distinct; mixing them shifts the stats anchor. Menlo's listed Stern acceptance rates (25%/31%/27%/19%/29%) are older years and differ from the Class-of-2027 24%, reflecting year-to-year volatility that any base rate must account for.

Test-optional/waiver impact is the weakest-evidenced area here (medium confidence, partly from search synthesis rather than a single authoritative page); the claim that 'most waiver-school admits still submit scores' is widely repeated but not tied to a hard, school-published statistic in these sources.
findings.benchmarks: NYU Stern calibration anchors for the stats gate and base rate. Class of 2027 (2024-25 cycle, entered fall 2025): overall admit rate ~24% (4,933 apps / 1,161 admits / 336 enrolled; yield 28.9%); GMAT 10th-edition avg 737, range 660-770; GMAT Focus avg 682, 80% range ~595-775; GRE avg ~327 (V163/Q164), 27% submitted GRE; GPA avg 3.64, full range 3.18-4.00, mid-80% ~3.36-3.89; 45% women; 43% international (sources: poetsandquants.com 2025-10-16; clearadmit.com 2025-10; stern.nyu.edu class profile). Class of 2026 comparison: 4,550 apps / 1,141 admits / 352 enrolled; GMAT avg 733; GPA 3.64; 47% women; 40% international. Menlo historical Stern overall acceptance rates: 25% (2024), 31% (2023), 27% (2022), 19% (2021), 29% (2020) — illustrating year-to-year base-rate volatility. Band mapping for output (ARINGO): stretch ~10%, competitive ~50%, strong+scholarship ~75-80%. Verbal-tier framework (Menlo): highly unlikely / reach / on-target / safe / practically guaranteed. Category rule of thumb (SBC + Menlo): TARGET = profile in middle-80% of admits (GMAT within ~10 pts, GPA within ~0.2 of avg); SAFETY = profile in ~top decile of admits; REACH = at/below median with differentiators or sub-15% acceptance-rate school. SBC distance heuristic: ~50 points below the 80%-range floor → drop a school tier. List construction (mbaMission): 5-7 schools split across a couple stretch / on-target / confident.

checks.claim: NYU Stern Class of 2027 core admissions metrics: 4,933 applications, 1,161 admits, ~24% admit rate, 336 enrolled, 28.9% yield.
checks.corrected: Confirmed exactly. Poets&Quants and Clear Admit both report 4,933 apps / 1,161 admits / 24% acceptance / 336 enrolled / 28.9% yield. Class of 2026 comparison (4,550 apps / 1,141 admits / 25% / 352 enrolled / ~30.9% yield) also confirmed via P&Q's own comparison table.

checks.claim: Stern Class of 2027 GMAT 10th-edition average 737 (record high, up from 733 for Class of 2026); GRE average ~327 (V163/Q164); GPA average 3.64; 45% women; 43% international (43 countries).
checks.corrected: All confirmed. GMAT 10th-ed avg 737 (Class of 2026 = 733); GRE V163/Q164 (avg ~327); GPA 3.64; 45% women; 43% international across 43 countries. The official Stern class-profile page lists the same figures and is currently showing the Class of 2027.

checks.claim: Stern Class of 2027 GMAT 10th-edition '80% range' is 660-770.
checks.corrected: 660-770 is the FULL/100% range (P&Q labels it 'range', Class of 2026 = 650-780), NOT the 80% range. The official Stern class-profile page gives the GMAT 10th-edition 80% range as 690-760. The draft conflates the full range with the 80% range; the correct 80% (mid-80%) range to use for a stats gate is 690-760.

checks.claim: Stern Class of 2027 GMAT Focus average 682 with an '80% range ~595-775'.
checks.corrected: Focus average 682 is correct, but 595-775 is the 100% (full) range, NOT the 80% range. The GMAT Focus 80% (middle-80%) range is 645-725. The draft mislabels the full range as the 80% range — this materially overstates the width of the stats gate (a ~595 Focus score is at the 1st-percentile floor of admits, not inside the middle 80%). Use 645-725 as the Focus 80% calibration band.

checks.claim: Stern Class of 2027 GPA: 80% range ~3.18-4.00, mid-80% ~3.36-3.89.
checks.corrected: The draft's labels are inverted/muddled. Per the official Stern page and P&Q: 3.18-4.00 is the FULL/100% range; 3.36-3.89 is the 80% (middle-80%) range. So the single correct 80% range is 3.36-3.89 (not 3.18-4.00). The numbers exist but the draft applies the '80%' label to the wrong pair.

checks.claim: Stern Class of 2027 test distribution: 27% submitted GRE; plus implied GMAT majority. (Draft cites 27% GRE.)
checks.corrected: Confirmed and expandable: 39% GMAT, 27% GRE, 6% Executive Assessment, 5% MCAT/LSAT/DAT, 16% secured a test waiver, and 7% were eligible for the NYU (undergrad GPA 3.20+) test waiver. The draft only mentioned 27% GRE; the fuller distribution is worth carrying because the 16% waiver share is directly relevant to the test-waiver penalty logic.

checks.claim: No-test/test-waiver profiles are treated as a conditional penalty; the draft frames Stern within generic 'waiver-offering school' dynamics. (FT-vs-part-time test-waiver distinction.)
checks.corrected: The draft never actually states the Stern-specific FT-vs-part-time distinction the task flags, and treats Stern as generically 'test-optional/waiver-offering', which is wrong for the Full-Time MBA. Correct picture: (1) Full-Time MBA and Luxury & Retail MBA are NOT test-optional — a score is REQUIRED unless a waiver is approved via a demonstrated-academic-readiness request (form + resume + transcripts + brief essay), reviewed rolling, decision usually within 3 weeks; waiver-request deadline ~April 1. (2) The Andre Koo Tech MBA IS genuinely test-optional. (3) The Part-Time/Langone MBA is test-required-unless-waived but via four CATEGORICAL pathways (U.S. business major GPA 3.20+, U.S. engineering major GPA 3.20+ with committee approval, NYU undergrad GPA 3.20+, and automatic U.S. military/veteran). (4) Across all programs, an NYU undergrad with cumulative GPA 3.20+ is auto-exempt from submitting a score. So 'Stern test waiver' behavior differs sharply by program; a predictor must not treat the Full-Time MBA as test-optional.

checks.claim: Current Stern Full-Time MBA essay prompts and limits: Essay 1 'Professional Aspirations' 500-word max; Essay 2 'Pick Six' (intro ≤3 sentences + six images + one-sentence captions); Essay 3 'Additional Information' optional 500-word max.
checks.corrected: Confirmed verbatim against the official Stern essays page. Essay 1: 'What are your short-term career goals? Why is the Stern MBA the necessary next chapter in your professional story? Please be specific.' (500 words, double-spaced, 12pt). Essay 2 'Pick Six': brief intro (no more than 3 sentences) + six images + one-sentence caption each. Essay 3 optional additional information (500 words). These are the prompts in effect for the 2026-2027 cycle.

checks.claim: NYU Stern Full-Time MBA 2026-2027 application deadlines (the draft did not list specific dates; verifying the cycle the research targets).
checks.corrected: Officially published on Stern's deadlines page for the cycle entering Fall 2027 (i.e., 2026-2027 cycle): Round 1 due Sep 15, 2026 (notify by Dec 1, 2026); Round 2 due Oct 15, 2026 (notify by Jan 1, 2027); Round 3 due Jan 15, 2027 (notify by Apr 1, 2027); Round 4 due Apr 15, 2027 (ongoing). All due 11:59 p.m. ET. Note: one consultant page (PersonalMBACoach, captured ~June 1, 2026) still said deadlines were unpublished — that snapshot is now stale; Stern's official page and Clear Admit both show the confirmed dates above.

checks.claim: ARINGO's calculator uses ~40+ categorical inputs, rates essays/recs/resume strong/medium/weak, and maps to bands: 'stretch' ~10%, 'competitive' ~50%, 'strong with scholarship potential' ~75-80%, explicitly a 'rough estimate only' that 'does not guarantee actual outcomes.'
checks.corrected: Confirmed. The ARINGO calculator page enumerates the input categories (GPA bands, GMAT/GRE/GMAT Focus, undergrad prestige, master's, work experience, people managed, brand/government/military employer, promotions, age, gender, awards, community service, sports, student government, entrepreneurship, international context), rates essays/recs/resume Strong/Medium/Weak, lets you set round (first/second/last), and characterizes outcomes as Stretch (~10%), Competitive (~50%), and Strong-with-scholarship (~75-80%), stating it 'provides a rough estimate only. It does not guarantee actual outcomes.'

checks.claim: Menlo Coaching lists NYU Stern historical overall acceptance rates of 25% (2024), 31% (2023), 27% (2022), 19% (2021), 29% (2020) and cautions the rate is 'not an indicator, one way or another, of your fit.'
checks.corrected: Confirmed on the cited general acceptance-rates page: Stern 25%/31%/27%/19%/29% for 2024-2020, with the verbatim caution 'they are certainly not an indicator, one way or another, of your fit with a program.' Caveat for the predictor: Menlo's school-specific Stern page now shows a single figure of 25% sourced to 'NYU Stern Class of 2027 data & U.S. News' — i.e., Menlo's own pages give slightly different headline rates depending on page/source (25% vs the P&Q-derived 24%), reinforcing the draft's point that the base rate is volatile and should be treated as a band, not a fixed number.

checks.claim: Menlo rejects single-number probability outputs as 'false precision' and uses five verbal tiers (highly unlikely / reach / on-target / safe / practically guaranteed); SBC's top rejection reasons are mostly story/positioning; cycle labeling Class of 2027 (entered fall 2025) vs Class of 2026 must be kept distinct.
checks.corrected: The methodological/qualitative claims (Menlo's verbal-tier framing and anti-false-precision stance; SBC's positioning-heavy rejection reasons; the dream/reach-target-safety logic) are consistent with the cited firm pages and were not contradicted by any source checked. Confidence is medium only because these are interpretive/qualitative positions rather than hard published figures, so exact wording can shift as firms update pages. The cycle-labeling point is correct and important: Class of 2027 = entered fall 2025 (2024-25 cycle); Class of 2026 GMAT 733 vs Class of 2027 GMAT 737 — do not mix the two stats anchors.

verify.netAssessment: The draft's methodology synthesis is sound and its core Class-of-2027 anchor numbers are accurate, but it contains a recurring, calibration-relevant labeling error on score RANGES and understates the Stern-specific test-waiver picture. CONFIRMED with high confidence: the headline Class-of-2027 metrics (4,933 apps / 1,161 admits / ~24% / 336 enrolled / 28.9% yield; GMAT 737; GRE ~327; GPA 3.64; 45% women; 43% international), the Class-of-2026 comparison (GMAT 733), the current essay prompts and 500-word limits, the published 2026-27 deadlines (R1 Sep 15 2026 → R4 Apr 15 2027), the ARINGO calculator inputs/bands/disclaimer, and the Menlo historical Stern acceptance-rate series with its 'not an indicator of your fit' caution. CORRECTED (three range errors, all the same mistake — full range mislabeled as 80% range): (1) GMAT 10th-edition 80% range is 690-760, not 660-770 (660-770 is the full range); (2) GMAT Focus 80% range is 645-725, not 595-775 (595-775 is the full range) — this is the most consequential because it nearly doubles the apparent width of the stats gate; (3) GPA 80% range is 3.36-3.89, with 3.18-4.00 being the full range. A predictor using the draft's ranges as the '80% gate' would treat scores at the 1st-percentile floor as 'inside the middle 80%' and badly miscalibrate the academics threshold. CORRECTED on the flagged FT-vs-part-time test-waiver distinction: the draft treats Stern generically as a 'waiver-offering/test-optional' school, but the Full-Time MBA is NOT test-optional (score required unless a readiness-essay waiver is granted); only the Andre Koo Tech MBA is genuinely test-optional; the Part-Time/Langone program uses four categorical waiver pathways; and any NYU undergrad with GPA 3.20+ is auto-exempt. The qualitative consultant-methodology claims (Menlo verbal tiers, SBC positioning-weighted rejections, dream/target/safety logic) are consistent with sources but inherently interpretive (medium confidence). Net: trust the methodology and the base-rate/average anchors; replace every '80% range' figure with the corrected pairs above; and rewrite the test-waiver section to distinguish Full-Time (required-unless-waived) from Tech MBA (test-optional) from Langone (categorical waivers).
